meta.title: UK Politics Fact-Check League
meta.description: A daily fact-check of the most-discussed claim in UK politics, scored on a 0-100 truth meter, with a running league table of everyone featured.
meta.note: Every figure below is anchored to a named primary or established secondary source (linked). Where a claim's accuracy depends on the time period or a definition, that is stated explicitly. Verdicts should be re-confirmed against the linked sources before publication.

entries.topic: Has this government cut net migration by 82%?
entries.claim: I am very proud that my government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party (until 20 July 2026); MP for Holborn and St Pancras
entries.discussionNote: As Andy Burnham's coronation as Labour leader and Prime Minister-in-waiting dominated the 9-13 July news cycle, the migration exchange at what was one of Keir Starmer's last Prime Minister's Questions (9 July 2026) was the other line that travelled. Pressed by Reform's Lee Anderson - flanked by Nigel Farage and Richard Tice - on whether he would deport every illegal immigrant, Starmer answered that he was 'very proud that my government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent', and the '82%' figure has since been recycled by Labour figures (the Home Secretary used the same number in May, and party chair Anna Turley repeated it) and rebutted by fact-checkers. The exchange was written up by the New Statesman ('Starmer and Badenoch spar over defence funding'), The London Economic ('Keir Starmer torches Lee Anderson at PMQs'), GB News and the Spectator. It is a clean, checkable comparison - a single percentage fall in ONS net-migration estimates, with a clear dispute over the baseline and who gets the credit - testable against the ONS long-term international migration release, the House of Commons Library and two dedicated Full Fact checks. It is a fresh subject (immigration) distinct from the recent G7-growth, fiscal-centralisation and defence-funding entries, even though the quote comes from the same PMQs as the 12 July growth claim.
entries.explanation: The number Starmer quoted is arithmetically right, but the ownership he claimed for it is not, which is exactly why this lands in Half True / Needs Context rather than higher. What is true: ONS estimates put net migration at 171,000 in the year ending December 2025, down from a revised peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 - a fall of about 82% (773,000 / 944,000 = 81.9%). So '82%' is a real, sourced figure. What Starmer gets wrong, and what pulls the score down, is attribution. That 82% is measured from a peak that occurred in March 2023, more than a year before Labour took office in July 2024, and under the previous Conservative government. Full Fact's dedicated checks make the point explicitly: the 82% is the fall 'from its peak in March 2023, not since Labour took office'. Measured from when Labour actually took office - the year ending June 2024 - net migration has fallen by about 74%, not 82%, so a meaningful slice of the total decline had already happened before this government arrived. And even the fall that has occurred on Labour's watch is not solely Labour's doing: the ONS says the main driver was a drop in non-EU nationals arriving for work, that the decline was 'may be partly' due to visa rule changes introduced in 2024 and 2025, and that projections already pointed to a fall from the 2023 record before Labour announced its own measures. The Migration Observatory concludes the fall 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations' - the Conservatives' 2024 restrictions (higher skilled-worker salary thresholds, dependant bans on students and care workers), which Labour kept, plus Labour's own 2025 changes (ending overseas care-worker recruitment, trimming the Skilled Worker job list). So the honest reading is: the 82% figure is correct, most of the fall has taken place since Labour took office, but the peak-to-trough framing lets Starmer claim credit for a decline that began earlier and rests substantially on Conservative-era policy plus a pre-existing downward trend. Because the headline number is accurate but the 'my government has brought down' framing overstates Labour's specific contribution, this is Half True / Needs Context.
evidence: Claim/context (PMQs, 9 July 2026): answering Reform's Lee Anderson on illegal immigration, Starmer said 'I am very proud that my government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent', at one of his last PMQs before stepping down as Labour leader (New Statesman; The London Economic; GB News).

Primary data (ONS, long-term international migration): net migration was 171,000 in the year ending December 2025, down from 331,000 a year earlier (a 48% year-on-year fall), against a revised peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 (previously estimated 906,000 for year ending June 2023).
The arithmetic: 944,000 down to 171,000 is a fall of about 82% - so the figure Starmer used is accurate as a peak-to-latest comparison.

Attribution 1 - baseline predates Labour (Full Fact): the 82% is measured 'from its peak in March 2023, not since Labour took office'; measured from the year ending June 2024, when Labour took office, net migration has fallen about 74%.

Attribution 2 - both governments (ONS / Migration Observatory): the ONS says the fall 'may be partly' due to visa rule changes introduced in 2024 and 2025 and that a fall was already projected before Labour's measures; the Migration Observatory says it 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations'.

Policy split: the Conservatives' 2024 restrictions (higher skilled-worker salary threshold, bans on dependants for students and care workers) were maintained by Labour, which added its own 2025 changes (ending overseas care-worker recruitment, cutting the Skilled Worker eligible-jobs list).
entries.confidence: High on the numbers, high on the attribution point. The 171,000 (year ending December 2025) and 944,000 peak (year ending March 2023) figures come directly from the ONS long-term international migration estimates, and the 82%/74% distinction and both-governments attribution come from two dedicated Full Fact checks plus the ONS's own commentary and the Migration Observatory. Starmer's exact wording is taken from New Statesman, The London Economic and GB News reporting of the 9 July 2026 PMQs rather than a directly fetched Hansard transcript (Hansard and several outlets return 403s to automated fetching), so the precise phrasing is press-sourced, though it is consistent across outlets. The main judgement call is the band: the 82% is a genuine, correctly calculated figure, which keeps it well above the False bands, but the 'my government has brought down' framing claims credit for a peak-to-trough fall that began under the Conservatives and rests substantially on Conservative-era policy - hence Half True / Needs Context rather than Mostly True.

entries.topic: Does the UK have 'the fastest-growing economy in the G7'?
entries.claim: In the first quarter of this year we had the fastest-growing economy in the G7, growth upgraded by the IMF and the OECD, unemployment down and inflation down.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party (until 20 July 2026); MP for Holborn and St Pancras
entries.discussionNote: With Andy Burnham's coronation as Labour leader and Prime Minister-in-waiting dominating the 9-12 July news cycle, the second-biggest Westminster story was the outgoing Prime Minister's valedictory defence of the economic record he and Chancellor Rachel Reeves are handing over. At Prime Minister's Questions on 9 July 2026 - the same set-piece where Kemi Badenoch attacked the Defence Investment Plan - Starmer answered Badenoch's charge that Reeves had 'killed economic growth' by insisting the UK had 'the fastest-growing economy in the G7' in the first quarter, with forecasts upgraded by the IMF and OECD, unemployment down and inflation down. The exchange was reported across the weekend by the Yorkshire Post ('Sir Keir Starmer defends Rachel Reeves' record from Badenoch attacks at PMQs'), PA and GB News, and the same 'fastest-growing economy in the G7' line is now being deployed by both Labour and the Conservatives (who claim it as their legacy) - making it the single most-repeated checkable macroeconomic boast in circulation. It is a clean, checkable comparison - real GDP growth ranked against the other six G7 members - testable against the ONS Q1 2026 GDP release, the House of Commons Library's GDP international comparisons briefing and Full Fact's standing government tracker on 'highest sustained growth in the G7'. It is a fresh speaker (Starmer) and a fresh subject (headline GDP growth) distinct from the recent Burnham fiscal-centralisation and Badenoch defence-funding entries.
entries.explanation: The specific fact Starmer cited is accurate for the quarter he named, but it is deployed as a general verdict on the state of the economy that the wider and more recent data do not support - which is why this sits in the Half True / Needs Context band rather than higher. What is true: the ONS's first quarterly estimate, published on 14 May 2026, put UK real GDP growth at 0.6% in the first quarter of 2026 (January to March), driven mainly by services (up 0.8%). That 0.6% was the highest quarterly growth of any G7 economy that had reported for the quarter - ahead of the United States (about 0.5%), Canada and Japan (about 0.4%), Germany (about 0.3%), Italy (about 0.2%) and France (about 0%). So 'in the first quarter of this year we had the fastest-growing economy in the G7', taken literally and time-bounded to Q1 as Starmer framed it, is correct. What needs context, and pulls the score down: first, it is a single quarter, not a trend. The government's own flagship pledge is the 'highest SUSTAINED growth in the G7', and Full Fact's standing tracker rates that pledge as off track - the UK topped the G7 on GDP per head in only one quarter (Q1 2025) since Labour took office, and on the latest comparable quarter (Q4 2025) had the joint second-lowest growth in the group. Second, momentum has since reversed: monthly ONS data show the economy contracted by 0.1% in April 2026, so by the time Starmer spoke in July the most recent hard data pointed the other way. Third, 'fastest-growing' on total GDP is flattered by the UK's faster population growth; on GDP per head - the measure the government itself prefers - the UK's G7 ranking is far less favourable. Fourth, the bundled sub-claims are softer than the headline: growth forecasts were nudged up by the IMF and OECD earlier in 2026 but from low bases and with warnings, and 'unemployment down, inflation down' are directional claims sensitive to the exact comparison period. Because the core G7 comparison is genuinely true for the quarter Starmer specified, but is being used to imply a sustained strength that the per-head data, the off-track 'sustained growth' pledge and the April contraction all undercut, this is Half True / Needs Context rather than Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (PMQs, 9 July 2026): defending Rachel Reeves against Badenoch's charge that the Budget 'killed economic growth', Starmer said the UK had 'the fastest-growing economy in the G7' in the first quarter, with growth upgraded by the IMF and OECD, unemployment down and inflation down (Yorkshire Post; PA; GB News).

Primary data (ONS, published 14 May 2026): UK real GDP grew 0.6% in Q1 2026 (January to March), the strongest quarterly expansion since Q1 2025, led by services (+0.8%); monthly GDP was flat in January, +0.4% in February and +0.3% in March.

G7 ranking (Q1 2026): the UK's 0.6% was the fastest of the G7 members that had reported - versus the US ~0.5%, Canada ~0.4%, Japan ~0.4%, Germany ~0.3%, Italy ~0.2% and France ~0% - so the 'fastest in the G7' claim is correct for that quarter (The London Economic; CNBC).

Context 1 - not sustained (Full Fact government tracker): the government's 'highest sustained growth in the G7' pledge 'appears off track'; the UK led the G7 on GDP per head in only one quarter (Q1 2025) since July 2024 and had the joint second-lowest growth in the G7 in Q4 2025.
Context 2 - momentum reversed: ONS monthly data show UK GDP contracted by 0.1% in April 2026, so the most recent hard data at the time Starmer spoke pointed downward, not up.

Context 3 - per head vs total: 'fastest-growing' on total GDP is partly a function of faster UK population growth; on GDP per head - the government's own preferred metric - the UK's recent G7 ranking is markedly weaker (Full Fact; House of Commons Library GDP international comparisons).
entries.confidence: High on the numbers, medium on the exact quote. The 0.6% Q1 2026 figure and its composition come directly from the ONS first quarterly estimate (14 May 2026); the G7 ranking for the quarter is corroborated by The London Economic and CNBC; and the 'not sustained', per-head and April-contraction caveats come from Full Fact's standing government tracker and the House of Commons Library's GDP international comparisons briefing. Starmer's exact wording is taken from Yorkshire Post/PA and GB News reporting of the 9 July 2026 PMQs rather than a directly fetched Hansard transcript (Hansard, the Commons Library and the Yorkshire Post all returned 403s to automated fetching), so the precise phrasing of the quote is press-sourced, though the substance is consistent across outlets. The main judgement call is the band: the Q1 G7 comparison is literally true as Starmer time-bounded it, which keeps it well above the False bands, but it is being used to imply an ongoing strength that the off-track 'sustained growth' pledge, the weaker per-head ranking and the 0.1% April contraction all qualify - hence Half True / Needs Context rather than Mostly True.

entries.topic: Are the UK's public finances 'the most centralised in the G7'?
entries.claim: With just 6% of taxes and 20% of spending devolved sub-nationally, the UK's public finances have been the most centralised in the G7.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield; presumptive winner of the Labour leadership election and Prime Minister-in-waiting, expected in No 10 on 20 July 2026
entries.discussionNote: Andy Burnham's imminent arrival in Downing Street was the dominant UK-politics story of 9-11 July 2026: on 9 July, the first day of the Labour leadership nominations, 322 of the 403 Labour MPs - close to four-fifths of the parliamentary party - nominated him, leaving him all but certain to become Labour leader and Prime Minister on 20 July without a members' ballot (Bloomberg, Al Jazeera, IBTimes, NBC). The whole news cycle was framed around his 'good growth in every postcode' devolution agenda, and the factual claim doing the heaviest lifting in that coverage is his assertion that the UK's public finances are 'the most centralised in the G7' - the diagnosis on which his entire 'No 10 North' pitch rests (CNBC 'No 10 North', Time, Business Standard, Bloomberg). Burnham set the claim out in his set-piece economic speech at the People's History Museum in Manchester on 29 June 2026 and has reiterated it through the nomination period as the justification for shifting tax and spending power out of Whitehall. It is a clean, checkable fiscal-comparison claim (share of tax and spending devolved sub-nationally, benchmarked across the G7 and OECD), testable against the OECD Fiscal Decentralisation Database, House of Commons Library material and independent analysis from the Centre for Cities and the Institute for Government. It is a fresh subject (fiscal centralisation / devolution) distinct from the recent defence-funding, migration and Farage-membership headlines, and a different Burnham claim from the earlier NEET entry.
entries.explanation: Burnham's core claim - that the UK's public finances are the most centralised in the G7 - is accurate and is independently endorsed by the very analysts who study it, which is why this sits firmly in Mostly True; it stops just short of the True band only because one of his figures is rounded slightly high and because 'most centralised' is a superlative that holds cleanly for the G7 (his stated frame) but not for every possible definition or wider comparator group. The substance: in the UK only about 5-6% of tax revenue is raised by sub-national government (metro mayors and local councils) and roughly 20% of public spending is controlled sub-nationally, against OECD averages of about 30% of tax and 38% of spending - so around 95% of tax flows straight to the Treasury in Whitehall. The OECD's own data put UK central government's share of general-government revenue at about 91.8% in 2023, versus an OECD average near 53%, placing the UK among the most centralised countries in the OECD and the most centralised in the G7. The Centre for Cities states plainly that 'Andy Burnham is right that the UK is the most fiscally centralised country in the G7', with just 5% of tax revenue going to local authorities and mayors, sub-national government the most grant-dependent of any G7 country (about 62% of its funding from central grants), and the UK roughly twice as centralised as the next G7 country, Italy; the Institute for Government reaches the same conclusion in its international comparison. What keeps it out of the True band: first, Burnham's quoted '6% of taxes' rounds up the roughly 5% figure that the Centre for Cities and OECD use - a small overstatement that does not change the ranking. Second, 'the most centralised' is a superlative tied to a specific metric (tax and spending shares) and to the G7 as the comparator; broadened to the whole 'developed world' or OECD it becomes an over-claim, because a handful of smaller countries (for example Ireland) are comparably centralised on some measures - but Burnham's own words say 'in the G7', which is the defensible framing. Third, the number is a genuine fiscal fact; the further, more contestable step - that this centralisation is what causes the UK's regional economic gaps - is a separate causal argument not scored here. Because the central comparison is correct, correctly framed to the G7, and confirmed by the OECD, the Centre for Cities and the Institute for Government, with only a minor figure round-up and the usual superlative caveat, this is Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (29 June 2026, reiterated through the 9 July leadership win): in his economic speech at the People's History Museum, Manchester, Burnham said that 'with just 6% of taxes and 20% of spending devolved sub-nationally, the UK's public finances have been the most centralised in the G7', the foundation of his 'good growth in every postcode' devolution agenda (Bloomberg; Yahoo Finance; Business Standard).

Independent confirmation (Centre for Cities): 'Andy Burnham is right that the UK is the most fiscally centralised country in the G7' - just 5% of tax revenue goes to local authorities and mayors, sub-national government is the most grant-dependent of any G7 country (about 62% of its funding from central grants, only ~19% from local taxes), and the UK is roughly twice as centralised as the next G7 country, Italy.

Primary data (OECD): UK central government raised about 91.8% of general-government revenue in 2023, against an OECD average of roughly 53% - among the most centralised in the OECD and the highest in the G7 (OECD Fiscal Decentralisation Database).

Benchmarks: about 5-6% of tax and ~20% of spending are devolved sub-nationally in the UK, versus OECD averages of roughly 30% of tax and 38% of spending (Centre for Cities; Institute for Government international comparison).

Caveats keeping it out of the True band: Burnham's '6%' rounds up the ~5% local-tax share; 'the most centralised' is a superlative that holds for the G7 (his stated frame) but is an over-claim if widened to the whole 'developed world'/OECD, where a few smaller states are comparably centralised; and the implied causal link between centralisation and regional inequality is a separate, more contested argument.
entries.confidence: High on the numbers, medium on the superlative's scope. The core figures - roughly 5-6% of tax and ~20% of spending devolved sub-nationally, ~95% of tax to Whitehall, UK central government's ~91.8% revenue share in 2023, and the UK as the most fiscally centralised G7 country and most grant-dependent - are consistent across the OECD Fiscal Decentralisation Database, the Centre for Cities (which explicitly says Burnham 'is right') and the Institute for Government. Burnham's exact wording is taken from Bloomberg/Yahoo/Business Standard reporting of the 29 June People's History Museum speech rather than a directly fetched transcript (several outlets returned 403s to automated fetching), but the phrasing is consistent across them. The main judgement calls are the small '6% vs ~5%' round-up and the scope of 'most centralised': it is solidly true for the G7 as Burnham framed it, but would be an overstatement if extended to the entire developed world - hence Mostly True rather than True.

entries.topic: Does Starmer's Defence Investment Plan have a '£4.7bn black hole' that Burnham will have to find £5bn to fill?
entries.claim: Did the member for Makerfield know that he was going to have to find £5bn for the Prime Minister's plan? ... the gulf between the £15bn promised and the £28bn [the Chief of the Defence Staff] wanted ... a plan with a £4.7bn black hole in it.
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party; MP for North West Essex
entries.discussionNote: This was the set-piece of Prime Minister's Questions on 9 July 2026: Badenoch used all six of her questions on defence funding, and the exchange dominated the Westminster news cycle across 9-10 July. The New Statesman ran a full 'PMQs Review: Starmer and Badenoch spar over defence funding', the Spectator ('Badenoch attacks Starmer over defence at PMQs'), GB News ('Lame duck Keir Starmer passes buck to Andy Burnham'), Yahoo/PA ('Badenoch torpedoes Starmer at PMQs') and the Yorkshire Post all led on it, and the £4.7bn gap in the Defence Investment Plan has been a running story since the plan was published on 30 June 2026 and since Defence Secretary John Healey resigned over the settlement in June. The claim is a clean, directly checkable set of numbers - £15bn promised, £28bn sought, £4.7bn unfunded, £5bn for Burnham to find - testable against the government's own Defence Investment Plan funding explainer, the House of Commons Library briefing (CBP-10935), the IFS response and IISS analysis. It targets a fresh subject and a fresh speaker (Badenoch, and defence funding) distinct from the recent Starmer migration headline, the Farage membership entry and the Burnham NEET entry, and it puts the plan the incoming Prime Minister is about to inherit squarely in the dock on the day it dominated the Commons.
entries.explanation: The core figure Badenoch built her attack on is exactly right and comes from the government's own paperwork, which is why this sits in Mostly True; it stops short of the True band only because '£5bn' rounds the number up and 'black hole' frames a deferred funding decision more dramatically than the facts strictly support. The numbers: the Defence Investment Plan, published on 30 June 2026, headlines a £15bn increase in the Ministry of Defence's spending power over the four years 2026-27 to 2029-30 (total planned defence spending of about £298bn). The government's own funding explainer states that of that £15bn, £10.3bn has been 'identified now' by reallocating budgets across departments, while 'a further £4.7bn over four years will be confirmed at Budget 2026' - including £1.8bn needed in 2026-27 alone. So Badenoch's '£4.7bn' is precisely the government's own unfunded figure, not an invented one. The IFS independently confirms that the government has not set out how it will pay for around a third of the increase - an average of about £1.2bn a year - and warns of 'further impacts on other areas of spending, tax or borrowing'; the IISS found only £11.5bn of the £15bn is genuinely new money, the other £3.4bn coming from 'spending power' measures such as asset sales. The £15bn-versus-£28bn gulf is also fair: service chiefs are widely reported to have sought around £28bn over the period and received roughly half, a point tied to Healey's resignation. And the Burnham angle is real: Andy Burnham, expected in Downing Street within weeks, does inherit the £4.7bn question, and has publicly vowed to 'ensure that it is fully funded'. What pulls it down from True: first, Badenoch's headline rhetorical figure was '£5bn', which rounds £4.7bn up by about £300m - fair as a round number but a slight overstatement. Second, 'black hole' implies money that has gone missing or been lost; more precisely, the £4.7bn is confirmed spending whose FUNDING SOURCE (reallocation, tax or borrowing) is deferred to Budget 2026 - it is 'confirmed but not funded', which is a genuine fiscal-credibility problem but not quite a hole in the everyday sense. Third, whether Burnham personally 'knew' is contested - a defence minister said he was not told about the gap - so that sub-claim is a live political dispute rather than an established fact. Because the central £4.7bn number is accurate, sourced to the government itself and corroborated by the IFS and IISS, with only the £5bn round-up and the loaded 'black hole' framing as caveats, this is Mostly True rather than True.
evidence: Claim/context (PMQs, 9 July 2026): Badenoch used all six questions on defence, asking 'Did the member for Makerfield know that he was going to have to find £5bn for the Prime Minister's plan?', contrasting '£15bn promised' with '£28bn' sought, and branding it a plan 'with a £4.7bn black hole in it' (New Statesman PMQs review; Spectator).

Core number (government's own funding explainer, gov.uk): the Defence Investment Plan confirms an additional £15bn for 2026-27 to 2029-30; £10.3bn is 'identified now', and 'a further £4.7bn over four years will be confirmed at Budget 2026' - so Badenoch's £4.7bn is the government's own unfunded figure, not an invention.
Next-year shortfall: £1.8bn of the £4.7bn is needed in 2026-27 and must be confirmed at Budget 2026 (gov.uk funding explainer; House of Commons Library CBP-10935; Portsmouth News).

Independent corroboration (IFS): the government has not set out how it will fund about a third of the £15bn increase - roughly £1.2bn a year - and this will mean 'further impacts on other areas of spending, tax or borrowing'; the IISS found only £11.5bn of the £15bn is new money (the rest from 'spending power' measures such as asset sales).

£15bn vs £28bn: the Chief of the Defence Staff / service chiefs are reported to have sought around £28bn over the four years and received roughly half, a shortfall tied to John Healey's resignation as Defence Secretary in June 2026 - so the 'gulf' framing is fair.

Caveats keeping it out of the True band: '£5bn' rounds £4.7bn up by about £300m; 'black hole' overstates what is a deferred funding decision ('confirmed but not funded') rather than lost money; and whether Burnham 'knew' is disputed - a defence minister said he was not told about the gap, while Burnham has vowed to 'fully fund' the plan.
entries.confidence: High on the numbers, medium on the framing. The £15bn increase, the £10.3bn funded now, the £4.7bn unfunded to Budget 2026 and the £1.8bn needed in 2026-27 all come directly from the government's own Defence Investment Plan funding explainer on gov.uk, and are corroborated by the House of Commons Library briefing (CBP-10935), the IFS response (about a third / £1.2bn a year unfunded; 'further impacts' on tax, borrowing or other spending) and IISS analysis (£11.5bn new money, £3.4bn from spending-power measures), plus consistent trade-press reporting (Portsmouth News, UK Defence Journal, Calibre Defence). Badenoch's wording is taken from the New Statesman and Spectator PMQs reviews and PA/Yahoo coverage rather than a directly fetched Hansard transcript (Hansard and the Commons Library returned 403s to automated fetching), so the exact '£5bn' and 'black hole' phrasing is sourced to those reports; the substance is consistent across all of them. The main judgement calls are the band and two sub-claims: the £4.7bn is unambiguously accurate, which lifts it above half-true, but '£5bn' rounds it up and 'black hole' is loaded, and whether Burnham personally 'knew' is a live dispute - so Mostly True rather than True.

entries.topic: Has the government brought net migration down by 82 per cent 'on the asylum system'?
entries.claim: I am very proud of the fact that after net migration reached almost one million under the party opposite, we brought it down by 82 per cent on the asylum system.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; MP for Holborn and St Pancras
entries.discussionNote: The exchange came at Prime Minister's Questions on 8 July 2026, when Reform UK MP Lee Anderson pressed the Prime Minister on illegal migration - asking whether every illegal migrant should be 'detained, not allowed to claim asylum and then be deported' - and Starmer replied with his 82 per cent boast. The clip was pushed out by BBC Politics on X and written up across ITV, GB News, The London Economic and London Business News, making it the most-shared migration soundbite of the 7-9 July window. Although Badenoch's £4.7bn defence-funding attack was the set-piece of the session, the 82 per cent figure is the more directly checkable claim: it is a single, superlative percentage, quoted verbatim, and it recurs almost daily from Labour ministers (the Home Secretary uses 'net migration has fallen by 82% in just three years'), so it underpins the government's whole migration record. It is testable against the ONS long-term international migration bulletin (year ending December 2025), the Migration Observatory, the House of Commons Library and a dedicated Full Fact analysis, and it targets a fresh subject distinct from yesterday's Farage membership headline, the Burnham NEET entry and the recent Starmer defence entries.
entries.explanation: The 82 per cent number is real and correctly quoted - but Starmer attached it to the wrong thing and to a period that is only partly his government's. The figure comes straight from the Office for National Statistics: net migration peaked at 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 (the pre-revision figure was 906,000) and had fallen to about 171,000 by the year ending December 2025. That is a fall of 81.9 per cent - so '82 per cent' and 'almost one million' at the peak are both fair. The problem is what the 82 per cent describes. It is a fall in overall NET MIGRATION (immigration minus emigration, dominated by work and study visas), not a fall 'on the asylum system'. Asylum is a small component of the total, and the asylum-specific numbers move differently: small-boat arrivals were down only about 13 per cent year-on-year to end-May 2026, the initial-decision backlog has roughly halved but the appeals backlog has doubled to near 70,000, and the Migration Observatory and Home Office data show asylum claims at or near record highs. So bolting '82 per cent' onto 'the asylum system' conflates two different things and flatters the asylum record. The second caveat is timing and credit. The 82 per cent is measured from the March 2023 peak - which fell under the Conservatives - not from when Labour took office. Full Fact's own working shows net migration fell 74 per cent under Labour alone (June 2024 to December 2025), with the remainder happening before the election; and the drivers were policy changes by BOTH governments - the Conservatives' late-2023/2024 visa restrictions (higher salary thresholds, the student-dependant ban) plus Labour's July 2025 tightening of care-worker recruitment and the Skilled Worker route. Saying 'we brought it down' claims sole credit for a trend that began under the previous government. So: the headline percentage is accurate and honestly sourced, which keeps it out of the false bands; but it is mislabelled as an asylum figure and over-attributed to Labour, which are material distortions rather than nuance - hence Half True / Needs Context.
evidence: Exact quote / context: at PMQs on 8 July 2026, responding to Reform MP Lee Anderson on illegal migration, Starmer said he was 'very proud' that 'after net migration reached almost one million under the party opposite, we brought it down by 82 per cent on the asylum system' (confirmed by BBC Politics and multiple write-ups).

Core number (ONS): net migration peaked at 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 (pre-revision 906,000) and fell to about 171,000 in the year ending December 2025 - a fall of 81.9 per cent, so '82 per cent' and 'almost one million' at peak are both accurate.

Mislabelling: the 82 per cent is a fall in overall NET MIGRATION (dominated by work/study), NOT 'on the asylum system'; Full Fact flags this exact error, noting immigration (arrivals) fell only about 45 per cent from peak, and asylum-specific measures differ - small-boat arrivals down only ~13 per cent year-on-year to end-May 2026 and asylum claims at or near record highs.

Timing / attribution: the 82 per cent is measured from the March 2023 peak, which fell under the Conservatives; Full Fact's figures show net migration fell 74 per cent under Labour alone (June 2024 to December 2025), and the fall was driven by visa policy changes from BOTH the previous Conservative government and Labour - so 'we brought it down' over-claims credit.

Fairness to Starmer: the underlying percentage is genuine, correctly quoted and directly sourced to the ONS, so this is not a fabricated or reversed figure - the fault is the label ('asylum system') and the sole-credit framing, not the number itself.
entries.confidence: High on the numbers, medium on interpretation. The peak (944,000, YE March 2023) and latest (~171,000-173,000, YE December 2025) net migration figures are consistent across the ONS bulletin, the ONS blog, the Migration Observatory and Full Fact, and 81.9 per cent from peak is a direct calculation. Full Fact independently corroborates both the mislabelling (net migration vs the asylum system, and vs immigration/arrivals) and the attribution point (74 per cent under Labour alone; drivers span both governments). Minor wobble: sources round the latest figure to 171,000 (ONS/Migration Observatory) or ~173,000 (Full Fact), which does not change the 82 per cent. The main judgement call is the band - the figure itself is accurate, which argues against a false verdict, but attaching it to 'the asylum system' and claiming sole credit are material distortions, which pulls it down to Half True / Needs Context rather than Mostly True.

entries.topic: Is Reform UK now the largest political party in Britain by membership?
entries.claim: Reform UK is the largest political party in Britain by membership, with more than 268,000 paid-up members - having overtaken Labour.
entries.speaker: Nigel Farage
entries.role: Leader of Reform UK; MP for Clacton until his 7 July 2026 resignation, which triggered a by-election he intends to contest as a 'people vs the establishment' fight
entries.discussionNote: Farage's shock resignation as MP for Clacton on 7 July 2026 - to force a by-election over Sunday Times revelations about undeclared benefits from convicted fraudster George Cottrell - was the dominant UK political story of the 6-8 July window, splashed by CNN, Al Jazeera, CNBC and Fox and framed by Farage as 'people vs the establishment'. His pitch rests on the claim that Reform is now the biggest force in British politics, and the party's signature, endlessly repeated 'largest party in Britain by membership' boast - first made when The Times, Bloomberg and LBC reported in December 2025 that Reform's counter had passed Labour's - was thrust back to the centre of the coverage. It is a discrete, superlative, directly checkable number, testable against Reform's own membership counter, Labour's audited annual-report figure, the House of Commons Library party-membership briefing (SN05125) and a dedicated UK Fact Check Politics analysis, and it targets a fresh subject distinct from the recent Starmer defence/migration entries, the Burnham NEET headline and the Badenoch defence entry - putting the claim underpinning Reform's whole 'insurgent front-runner' framing in the dock on the day its leader dominated the news.
entries.explanation: The headline is broadly supported by the reported numbers - Reform genuinely does appear to have more paid members than any other British party right now - which is why this sits in Mostly True; it falls to the low end of that band, and not into the True band, because the comparison is not like-for-like and the word 'largest' rests on two figures counted in fundamentally different ways. The numbers: Reform UK's own live membership counter passed 268,000 in late 2025 (LBC reported 268,631 'active members'), and The Times, Bloomberg and LBC all reported in December 2025 that this put Reform ahead of Labour, whose paid membership had slid below 250,000 after haemorrhaging roughly 100,000 members since the 2024 general election. On those figures Reform is the largest, and it is also comfortably ahead of the Conservatives, who last disclosed around 131,000 members at their 2024 leadership election and rarely publish a running total. The context that keeps it out of the True band: Reform's number is a self-reported, continuously updating website counter, while Labour's is an audited figure published in an annual report under constitutional membership rules. These are not the same thing. A live sign-up counter can capture new joiners instantly but lag on lapses and cancellations, so it may overstate the number of people who are actually paid-up at any given moment; 'paid-up' can also mean 'ever paid once' rather than 'currently in good standing'; and no independent audit has compared the two parties on a shared definition. That is why UK Fact Check Politics rated the specific claim 74/100 and called the comparison 'apples-to-oranges' rather than simply true. What is fair to Farage: when Reform first crossed the line, it invited the Financial Times, Sky News, The Spectator and The Telegraph to inspect its website code and underlying data, and those outlets reported the ticker to be accurate, so this is not a fabricated figure - Kemi Badenoch's earlier suggestion that the numbers were invented was not borne out. The claim is therefore a real, corroborated, plausibly-leading figure stated as a simple 'largest party' fact, with the genuine caveat that it compares a live counter against audited rolls; that is context rather than a factual error, so it is Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (resignation, 7 July 2026): Farage resigned as MP for Clacton to force a 'people vs the establishment' by-election after Sunday Times reports of undeclared benefits from convicted fraudster George Cottrell; the story led CNN, Al Jazeera, CNBC and Fox across 6-8 July, foregrounding Reform's claim to be the biggest party in Britain.

Core number (Reform's counter): Reform UK's live membership counter passed 268,000 in late 2025 - LBC reported 268,631 'active members' - the figure the party cites for its 'largest party in Britain' claim.

Comparison (Labour): The Times, Bloomberg and LBC reported in December 2025 that Labour's paid membership had fallen below 250,000, having lost roughly 100,000 members since the 2024 general election, putting Reform ahead on the reported figures.
Comparison (Conservatives): the Conservatives last disclosed around 131,000 members at their 2024 leadership election and rarely publish a running total, so Reform is clearly ahead of them too.

Key caveat (not like-for-like): Reform's figure is a self-reported live website counter, while Labour's is an audited figure in its annual report; a live counter can lag on cancellations and may overstate current paid-up membership, and 'paid-up' is defined differently by each party - so UK Fact Check Politics rated the comparison 'apples-to-oranges' (74/100).

Fairness / not fabricated: when it first crossed the line, Reform invited the FT, Sky News, The Spectator and The Telegraph to inspect its website code and data, and those outlets reported the ticker to be accurate; Badenoch's earlier claim that the figures were fabricated was not substantiated.
entries.confidence: Medium-high. The ~268,000 Reform figure (LBC: 268,631) and the sub-250,000 Labour figure are consistently reported by The Times, Bloomberg, LBC and Nation.Cymru for December 2025, and corroborated by a dedicated UK Fact Check Politics analysis and the Wikipedia Reform UK entry; the Conservatives' ~131,000 comes from their 2024 leadership election. The soft spots are real and are why this is not scored higher: both parties' totals are moving targets, Reform's counter is self-reported rather than audited (and the party disabled its public ticker in January 2026, so recent figures are partly estimated), Labour publishes only annually, and one secondary source (Wikipedia) carries an internally inconsistent forward-dated table showing Labour above Reform, which I have discounted in favour of the consistent December 2025 reporting from The Times, Bloomberg and LBC. I could not load the House of Commons Library party-membership briefing (SN05125) directly - it returned a 403 to automated fetching - so the Library figure is cited by title/URL rather than quoted. The main judgement call is the band: the numbers do support 'largest party by membership', which puts it above half-true, but the comparison mixes a live counter with audited rolls, so it is Mostly True at the low end rather than True.

entries.topic: Are 'almost a million' young people in Britain not in education, employment or training?
entries.claim: Almost a million young people out of education, work or training - that should be a source of national shame.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield; front-runner in the 2026 Labour leadership contest (nominations open 9 July), widely expected to become Prime Minister
entries.discussionNote: With the Labour leadership handover the dominant Westminster story of the 5-7 July window and Andy Burnham the overwhelming favourite to be Prime Minister once nominations open on 9 July, the signature statistic from his 'Manchesterism' relaunch - the roughly one million young people not in education, employment or training (NEET) that he has made the centrepiece of his domestic pitch - was thrust back to the top of the news cycle. His 29 June 'good growth in every postcode' speech at Manchester's People's History Museum foregrounded the NEET number, and the theme dominated the follow-on coverage across the 5-7 July window: the New Statesman's 6 July 'How to solve the Neet crisis', LabourList's 'Manchesterism at home and abroad', Youth Employment UK's write-up, Tees Business ('Tees leaders respond to Burnham NEETs promise') and Wonkhe all built on the same figure, and Bloomberg (3 July) noted Burnham's online reach now outstrips Farage's. It is a discrete, superlative, directly checkable number - testable against the ONS NEET bulletin (January to March 2026), the House of Commons Library NEET briefing (SN06705) and the Youth Employment UK data explainer - and it is a fresh subject distinct from the recent Starmer defence/economy/migration entries, the Farage ECHR entry and the Badenoch defence entry, putting the incoming Prime Minister's own headline domestic statistic in the dock.
entries.explanation: The headline number is accurate - if anything Burnham slightly understates it - which is why this sits comfortably in Mostly True; it stops short of the True band only because the NEET label bundles together several very different groups and 'out of education, work or training' invites the everyday inference that these are all idle or unemployed young people, when the official figure is broader than that. The number: the ONS estimates that 1,012,000 people aged 16 to 24 in the UK were NEET in Quarter 1 (January to March) 2026 - 13.5 per cent of that age group - which is the first time the total has exceeded one million since October to December 2013, when it stood at 1,038,000. So 'almost a million' is correct and, strictly, a touch conservative: the figure is just over a million, and Burnham's broader framing elsewhere of 'more than a million' is exact. The rise is real and recent: NEET numbers are up about 89,000 on the year and 55,000 on the quarter, with the NEET rate climbing from around 12 per cent at the end of 2023 to a new high of 13.5 per cent in Q1 2026. The context that keeps it out of the True band: 'NEET' is not a synonym for youth unemployment. Of the 1,012,000, about 400,000 are unemployed (looking for and available for work) while about 613,000 are economically inactive - a group that includes young people who are long-term sick or disabled, those with caring responsibilities, and some who are between courses or not in FULL-TIME education - and the ONS notes this is the highest level of economic inactivity among NEET young people since comparable records began in 2001. The measure also depends on the 'in education or training' definition (it counts any form of education or training, not just full-time study), and it is a Labour Force Survey estimate subject to sampling uncertainty, so the precise total can move on revision. What is fair to Burnham: the one-million figure is a genuine ONS statistic, correctly stated and if anything understated, the 'first time since 2013' milestone is real, and describing a decade-high of over a million young people out of education, work or training as a serious national problem is a legitimate reading of the data. The only reason this is not scored higher is the standard NEET caveat - that the total mixes the unemployed with the economically inactive and long-term sick, so it should not be read as a million idle jobseekers - which is context rather than a factual error, so it is Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (Manchester speech, 29 June 2026, dominant in the 5-7 July leadership coverage): Burnham made the roughly one-million NEET figure the centrepiece of his 'good growth in every postcode' relaunch, framing 'almost a million young people out of education, work or training' as a source of national shame; the theme ran across the New Statesman (6 July 'How to solve the Neet crisis'), LabourList, Youth Employment UK, Tees Business and Wonkhe.

Core number (ONS, NEET bulletin, January to March 2026): an estimated 1,012,000 young people aged 16 to 24 were NEET, 13.5 per cent of the age group - so 'almost a million' is correct and, strictly, slightly understates a figure that is just over one million.
Milestone: this is the first time the number of NEET young people has exceeded one million since October to December 2013, when it stood at 1,038,000 - so the 'decade-high' framing is accurate.
Recent rise: NEET numbers are up about 89,000 on the year and 55,000 on the quarter, with the NEET rate rising from around 12 per cent at the end of 2023 to a new high of 13.5 per cent in Q1 2026.

Key context (composition): of the 1,012,000, about 400,000 are unemployed while about 613,000 are economically inactive (including the long-term sick or disabled, those with caring responsibilities, and some not in full-time education) - the highest NEET economic-inactivity level since records began in 2001 - so NEET is broader than 'youth unemployment' and should not be read as a million idle jobseekers.

Measurement caveat: NEET counts anyone not in ANY form of education or training (not just full-time study) and is a Labour Force Survey estimate subject to sampling uncertainty, so the exact total can shift on revision - but on the published figure Burnham's number is right.
entries.confidence: High. The 1,012,000 total, the 13.5 per cent rate, the first-time-over-one-million-since-2013 milestone (1,038,000 in Oct-Dec 2013), the +89,000 year-on-year and +55,000 quarterly rises, and the 400,000-unemployed / 613,000-economically-inactive split all come directly from the ONS 'Young people not in education, employment or training (NEET), UK' bulletin (January to March 2026), corroborated by the House of Commons Library NEET briefing (SN06705), the Youth Employment UK quarterly data explainer and the New Statesman's 6 July NEET piece, which independently confirm the same figures and the 'highest economic inactivity since 2001' point. Burnham's framing of the number is attested across FE Week, LabourList, the New Statesman and Novara Media, though the precise 'national shame' wording is a close paraphrase in the coverage rather than a confirmed verbatim quote, so it is presented as his framing rather than a locked transcript line. The main judgement call is the band: the figure is genuinely correct and if anything understated, which puts it well above half-true, but the NEET label bundles the unemployed with the economically inactive and long-term sick, and 'out of education, work or training' invites the inference that these are all idle young people, which is context the headline number omits - so it is Mostly True rather than True. The one soft spot is that NEET is a survey estimate subject to revision and the exact total can move, though this does not change the assessment of the claim as made.

entries.topic: Will the government spend 'an extra £270 billion' on defence this Parliament?
entries.claim: We will spend £270 billion more than we would otherwise have done on defence in this Parliament.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister (resigned as Labour leader on 22 June 2026; remaining as Prime Minister pending the leadership transition, with nominations opening 9 July)
entries.discussionNote: As Westminster braced for the Labour leadership handover and Starmer's final major act as Prime Minister - the £300bn Defence Investment Plan (DIP) unveiled on 30 June 2026 - his signature defence-funding boast was thrust back to the centre of the news cycle and, on the same day, re-checked and rated 'Incorrect' again by Full Fact ('Defence spending not set to increase by an extra £270 billion'). The '£270 billion more' line - which Starmer put to Parliament's Liaison Committee on 23 March 2026 and which the government has recycled around every defence announcement since - is the single cleanest, most-repeated hard number attached to the DIP coverage that ran across Al Jazeera ('£300bn defence investment plan'), France 24 ('outgoing UK PM announces record defence spending'), LBC and Forces News over the 30 June-5 July window. It is a discrete, superlative, directly checkable figure - testable against the 2025 Spending Review line-by-line MoD budget, the House of Commons Library defence-spending briefing, defence minister Luke Pollard's own confirmation of what the number means, and Full Fact's two dedicated fact-checks - and it targets a different error from the recent defence entries: the 3 July entry checked Badenoch's '£28bn requirement / only half' attack and an earlier entry checked Starmer's 'biggest increase since the Cold War' framing, whereas this checks the specific £270bn total-vs-increase confusion that headline defence coverage still leans on.
entries.explanation: The £270 billion figure is real and comes from the government's own Spending Review, so this is not invented - but the load-bearing word in Starmer's sentence, 'more', turns a total budget into a claimed increase and inflates the genuine uplift by an order of magnitude, which is why Full Fact rated it flatly 'Incorrect' and why it lands in Mostly False rather than merely needing context. What the number actually is: totalling the Ministry of Defence's planned budget for each year from 2025/26 to 2028/29 - £62.2bn rising to £73.5bn - gives about £272.2bn, which is where '£270 billion' comes from. It is the MoD's cumulative TOTAL budget over four years, not the amount by which defence spending will rise. Defence minister Luke Pollard confirmed exactly this in February 2026, stating that £270bn 'is the total of the Ministry of Defence's budget from financial year 2025/26 to 2028/29'. Starmer's claim - 'we will spend £270 billion MORE than we would otherwise have done' - presents that entire four-year total as if it were extra money on top of a counterfactual, which it is not: almost all of the £270bn would have been spent on defence regardless. The genuine year-on-year increases are far smaller - about £1.9bn between 2024/25 and 2025/26 and a projected £3.3bn the following year - single-digit billions, not £270bn. On the government's own headline metric the real story is a rise in defence spending to 2.6% of GDP (NATO definition, including security and intelligence) by April 2027, on the way to a 3.5% NATO target by 2035; that is a real and substantial uplift, but describing the whole £270bn base as an increase overstates it many times over. What is fair to Starmer: the £270bn is a genuine Spending Review figure, defence spending is genuinely rising in cash and real terms, and the DIP announced on 30 June does add a further ~£15bn (about £11bn of it new spending between 2026/27 and 2028/29), taking the four-year total towards £300bn - so this is not False or Fabricated. But converting the MoD's total four-year budget into '£270 billion more than we would otherwise have done' is a material misrepresentation of what the figure measures - the same error the government has now been corrected on twice - which is Mostly False.
evidence: Claim/context (Liaison Committee, 23 March 2026, repeated around the 30 June Defence Investment Plan): Starmer told MPs the government 'will spend £270 billion more than we would otherwise have done on defence' in this Parliament; Full Fact rated the claim 'Incorrect' on 23 March and re-checked and re-rated it 'Incorrect' on 30 June 2026 as the DIP dominated coverage (Al Jazeera, France 24, LBC, Forces News).

What £270bn actually is (2025 Spending Review): defence spending is planned at about £62.2bn in 2025/26 rising to £73.5bn in 2028/29; summing the MoD's planned budget across 2025/26 to 2028/29 gives roughly £272.2bn - the MoD's cumulative TOTAL budget over four years, not the increase.

Minister's own confirmation: defence minister Luke Pollard confirmed in February 2026 that £270bn 'is the total of the Ministry of Defence's budget from financial year 2025/26 to 2028/29' - i.e. a total, not extra money 'more than we would otherwise have done'.
Real increases are far smaller: the genuine year-on-year rises are about £1.9bn between 2024/25 and 2025/26 and a projected £3.3bn the following year - single-digit billions, not a £270bn uplift.

The genuine uplift on the government's own metric: defence spending (NATO definition, including security and intelligence) is set to reach 2.6% of GDP by April 2027, with a committed path to a 3.5% NATO target by 2035 - a real, substantial increase, but nothing like £270bn of extra money.

Fresh top-up (Defence Investment Plan, 30 June 2026): Starmer's DIP adds a further ~£15bn over four years (about £11bn of it new spending between 2026/27 and 2028/29), taking the four-year total towards ~£300bn - so spending is genuinely rising, which keeps the claim above the False band.
entries.confidence: High. The £272.2bn four-year total, the £62.2bn-to-£73.5bn per-year budget path, the ~£1.9bn and ~£3.3bn real year-on-year increases, the 2.6%-of-GDP-by-2027 and 3.5%-by-2035 NATO commitments, and Luke Pollard's confirmation that £270bn 'is the total of the Ministry of Defence's budget from financial year 2025/26 to 2028/29' all come from the 2025 Spending Review, the House of Commons Library defence-spending briefing (CBP-8175) and Full Fact's two dedicated fact-checks, which independently establish that £270bn is a total rather than an increase and rate Starmer's 'more than we would otherwise have done' framing 'Incorrect'. Starmer's exact wording is taken from his 23 March 2026 Liaison Committee evidence; the 30 June £15bn/~£300bn Defence Investment Plan figures are corroborated across Al Jazeera, France 24, LBC and Forces News. The main judgement call is the band: because the £270bn is a genuine Spending Review figure and defence spending is genuinely rising, the claim is above the False band, but presenting the MoD's entire four-year total as money spent 'more than we would otherwise have done' misrepresents the figure by an order of magnitude - the same error corrected twice - which keeps it in Mostly False rather than Half True. The one soft spot is the exact size of the fresh DIP top-up (variously ~£15bn headline versus ~£11bn of genuinely new spend), which does not change the assessment of the £270bn claim itself.

entries.topic: Will migrants 'using the ECHR to stay in the UK' cost the taxpayer £5bn 'over the last year alone'?
entries.claim: The Home Office have just snuck out some figures they'd rather you didn't see. Over 34,000 people were given asylum status in Britain on the basis of Article 8 of the ECHR, the right to a family life. The estimated cost of those 34,000 people over their lifetimes to the British taxpayer is 5 billion pounds. Article 8, over the course of the last year alone, will cost the British taxpayer 5 billion pounds. Allowing those boats to cross the Channel is bankrupting Britain.
entries.speaker: Nigel Farage
entries.role: Leader of Reform UK; MP for Clacton
entries.discussionNote: As the Labour leadership handover dominated Westminster, the freshest hard, checkable number to break into the wider news cycle over the 30 June-4 July window was Nigel Farage's viral social-media video reacting to a Home Office paper on the fiscal cost of Article 8 (ECHR right to family/private life) grants. The '34,000 people / £4.9bn / £5bn' line was amplified across GB News ('Farage fumes after Home Office admits migrants using ECHR to avoid deportation will cost £4.9billion'), Breitbart ('Bankrupting Britain'), AOL/PA and MSN, and sits on top of the government's own Article 8 review and Shabana Mahmood's asylum-reform push - making it one of the highest-reach, most-shared factual claims of the window. It is a discrete number/comparison, directly testable against the Home Office's published 'Estimated lifetime net fiscal impact of Article 8 main applicants' analysis, and it is distinct from the recent Starmer (economy, migration), Badenoch (defence, welfare) and Burnham (devolution, living standards) entries, putting the Reform leader on the scoreboard.
entries.explanation: The two headline numbers Farage cites - about 34,000 people and roughly £5bn - are real and come from the Home Office's own published analysis, so this is not invented; but almost every framing device he wraps around them is wrong, and the load-bearing claim that this is a cost 'over the last year alone' inverts what the figure actually measures, which is why it lands in Mostly False rather than merely needing context. The numbers: the Home Office paper 'Estimated lifetime net fiscal impact of Article 8 main applicants' estimates that the 34,400 first-time in-country Article 8 main applicant grantees in 2025 carry an indicative net lifetime fiscal cost of about £4.9 billion, derived by multiplying that cohort by an assumed £141,000 net lifetime cost per person. So '34,000 people' and '£4.9bn/£5bn' are correctly lifted from the source. The first and most important distortion is timing: Farage says Article 8 'over the course of the last year alone, will cost the British taxpayer 5 billion pounds', but the £4.9bn is the estimated net fiscal effect of that cohort accumulated over their entire remaining lifetimes - decades of projected taxes minus public-service use, discounted to a present value - not money spent in the last year. Presenting a multi-decade lifetime model as a single year's cost is the central error. The second distortion is category: Article 8 is the right to private and family life, and the Home Office document explicitly does NOT say the cohort 'used the ECHR to avoid deportation', nor that they are all asylum seekers or people who 'crossed the Channel in boats'; these are first-time in-country Article 8 grantees processed through normal immigration routes, so Farage's splice of the figure onto 'allowing those boats to cross the Channel' is unsupported by the source. The third problem is precision: the Home Office repeatedly stresses the estimate is 'indicative and assumption-sensitive, not a precise measure', excludes dependants, excludes visa-fee and Immigration Health Surcharge income, ignores wider macroeconomic effects, and rests on decades-long assumptions about earnings, employment, settlement, emigration and mortality - so treating it as a hard £5bn bill overstates its solidity. What is fair to Farage: the £4.9bn and the ~34,400 figure are genuinely the Home Office's own numbers, he is right that the paper was published quietly alongside the asylum-reform package, and a lifetime net cost of that order is what the model produces - so this is not False or Fabricated. But he converts a caveat-laden, lifetime, present-value estimate into an annual 'last year alone' cost, reassigns a family-life immigration category to Channel-crossing asylum abuse, and drops every uncertainty the Home Office attached - three material misrepresentations of a real figure, which is Mostly False.
evidence: Claim/context (30 June 2026): in a widely shared social-media video, reacting to the Home Office's Article 8 fiscal paper, Farage said 'over 34,000 people were given asylum status... on the basis of Article 8', that 'the estimated cost of those 34,000 people over their lifetimes... is 5 billion pounds', that 'Article 8, over the course of the last year alone, will cost the British taxpayer 5 billion pounds', and that 'allowing those boats to cross the Channel is bankrupting Britain'; the line was amplified across GB News, Breitbart, AOL/PA and MSN over the 30 June-4 July window.

Source numbers (Home Office): the paper 'Estimated lifetime net fiscal impact of Article 8 main applicants' estimates about 34,400 first-time in-country Article 8 main applicant grantees in 2025, with an indicative net lifetime fiscal cost of roughly £4.9 billion, based on an assumed ~£141,000 net lifetime cost per person - so Farage's '34,000' and '£5bn' are correctly taken from the source.

Timing distortion (central error): the £4.9bn is the estimated present value of net fiscal effects accumulated over the cohort's remaining lifetimes (decades of projected taxes minus public-service use), NOT money spent in the last year - so 'over the last year alone, will cost the British taxpayer 5 billion pounds' misrepresents a lifetime model as a single year's spending.

Category distortion: Article 8 is the right to private and family life; the Home Office document does not describe the cohort as people who 'used the ECHR to avoid deportation', as asylum seekers, or as Channel 'boat' arrivals - they are first-time in-country Article 8 grantees via normal immigration routes, so splicing the figure onto 'those boats to cross the Channel' is unsupported.

Precision/caveats (Home Office): the estimate 'should be interpreted as indicative and assumption-sensitive, not as a precise measure', is 'subject to significant uncertainty', excludes dependants, excludes visa-fee and Immigration Health Surcharge income, ignores wider macroeconomic effects, and relies on long-term assumptions about earnings, employment, settlement, emigration and mortality - so a hard '£5bn bill' overstates its solidity.

Scale caveat: the 34,400 figure is specifically first-time in-country main applicants; the government's own asylum-reform material notes about 77,000 Article 8 applications granted in the past year, and the £4.9bn covers only the narrower main-applicant cohort and excludes dependants - so the figure is neither the whole Article 8 picture nor an annual cash cost.
entries.confidence: High. The 34,400 grantees, the ~£4.9bn indicative net lifetime fiscal cost, the ~£141,000 per-person figure and every caveat (indicative not precise, dependants excluded, visa-fee/IHS income excluded, wider macroeconomic effects ignored, decades-long assumptions) come directly from the Home Office's published 'Estimated lifetime net fiscal impact of Article 8 main applicants' analysis on GOV.UK, corroborated by the Preston Blog data explainer, which independently sets out that the £4.9bn is a lifetime present-value model rather than a year's spending and that the source does not classify the cohort as asylum seekers or people avoiding deportation. Farage's verbatim wording ('over the last year alone... 5 billion pounds'; 'allowing those boats to cross the Channel is bankrupting Britain') is reported in the Breitbart write-up of his 30 June video and echoed by GB News, AOL/PA and MSN. The main judgement call is the band: the £4.9bn and 34,400 are genuinely the Home Office's own figures, so this is well above the False band, but Farage converts a caveat-laden lifetime present-value estimate into an annual 'last year alone' cost, reassigns a family-life immigration category to Channel-crossing asylum abuse, and strips every uncertainty the Home Office attached - three material misrepresentations of a real number - which keeps it in Mostly False rather than Half True. The one soft spot is the exact per-person figure (variously reported as £141,000 and £141,100) and rounding of £4.9bn to '£5bn', neither of which changes the assessment.

entries.topic: Did the UK have 'the fastest-growing economy in the G7' in the first quarter of 2026?
entries.claim: In the first quarter of this year the UK had the fastest-growing economy in the G7. Our growth was upgraded by the IMF and the OECD, last week unemployment was down, inflation was better, that is because with this Chancellor we have the right economic plan and we can weather the global storms.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; former Leader of the Labour Party (resigned as Labour leader on 22 June 2026, remaining as Prime Minister pending the leadership transition)
entries.discussionNote: Prime Minister's Questions on 1 July 2026 was the dominant Westminster set-piece of the 1-4 July news cycle, and while the defence-funding exchange (checked in the 3 July entry) and the net-migration line (2 July entry) drew the sharpest attacks, the second running theme of the session was Kemi Badenoch's assault on Rachel Reeves's economic record and Starmer's headline rebuttal in her defence: that 'in the first quarter of this year the UK had the fastest-growing economy in the G7'. This growth boast is a discrete, superlative, directly checkable statistic that ran across the Yorkshire Post ('Starmer defends Reeves' record from Badenoch attacks'), GB News and the GOV.UK Downing Street write-up ('turning progress into results for working people'), was amplified by Labour MPs on social media ('GOOD NEWS! The UK was the fastest growing economy in the G7 for the first quarter'), and is testable directly against the ONS GDP first-quarterly-estimate bulletin (January to March 2026), the House of Commons Library GDP international-comparisons briefing and Full Fact's dedicated G7-growth government-tracker. It is a fresh economy/GDP claim distinct from the defence (3 July), migration (2 July) and welfare (1 July) entries, and puts the sitting Prime Minister's own economic framing in the dock.
entries.explanation: The load-bearing claim - that the UK had the fastest-growing economy in the G7 in the first quarter of 2026 - is accurate on the official data, which is why it sits in Mostly True; it stops short of the True band only because it cherry-picks a single strong quarter that was immediately followed by a contraction, and the 'upgraded by the IMF and the OECD' element is only half-right. The core number: the ONS GDP first-quarterly-estimate bulletin puts UK real GDP growth at 0.6 per cent in Quarter 1 (January to March) 2026, the highest of any G7 economy that had reported - ahead of the United States (0.5 per cent), Canada (0.4 per cent), Germany (0.3 per cent), Italy (0.2 per cent) and France (0.0 per cent), with Japan's figure still outstanding but estimated at around 0.4 per cent, which would still leave the UK top. So 'the fastest-growing economy in the G7' for that specific quarter is correct and correctly attributed to official statistics, and the ONS also notes UK real GDP per head rose 0.6 per cent, the fastest quarterly per-capita growth in four years. The IMF/OECD element is partly right: the IMF did upgrade its 2026 UK growth forecast, from 0.8 per cent to 1.0 per cent in its May 2026 update (citing data revisions and a robust Q1 outturn), so 'upgraded by the IMF' is accurate; but the OECD's 3 June 2026 forecast of 0.9 per cent for 2026 was a projection rather than a clear-cut upgrade, so bracketing 'the IMF and the OECD' together as having both 'upgraded' the UK slightly overstates the OECD half. The caveats that keep it out of the True band: first, this is a single-quarter snapshot - Full Fact's government-tracker rates Labour's 'highest sustained growth in the G7' pledge as 'appears off track', noting that one strong quarter is not sustained leadership, and the UK economy contracted by 0.1 per cent in April 2026, immediately after the quarter Starmer cited, so the momentum did not carry; second, the Q1 comparison rests on OECD data retrieved in mid-May 2026 that is subject to cross-country revision, and Japan had not yet reported. What is fair to Starmer: the specific claim he made - fastest-growing in the G7 in Q1 2026 - is true as stated, correctly measured and correctly sourced, and he did not claim sustained or full-year leadership in this line. With the single-quarter framing and the softer OECD half noted, the claim is accurate as used, so it is Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (PMQs, 1 July 2026): defending Rachel Reeves's record against Kemi Badenoch's attacks on the Budget, winter fuel and unemployment, Starmer said 'in the first quarter of this year the UK had the fastest-growing economy in the G7', with growth 'upgraded by the IMF and the OECD', unemployment down and inflation better; the economy was the second running theme of the session after defence (Yorkshire Post, GB News, GOV.UK Downing Street write-up).

ONS Q1 2026 figure (GDP first quarterly estimate, January to March 2026): UK real GDP grew 0.6 per cent in Q1 2026, the highest of the reporting G7 economies - US 0.5 per cent, Canada 0.4 per cent, Germany 0.3 per cent, Italy 0.2 per cent, France 0.0 per cent - with Japan (est. ~0.4 per cent) still to report, so the UK was fastest as stated; UK real GDP per head also rose 0.6 per cent, the fastest quarterly per-capita growth in four years.
IMF upgrade (May 2026): the IMF raised its 2026 UK growth forecast from 0.8 per cent to 1.0 per cent, citing data revisions and a robust Q1 outturn - so 'upgraded by the IMF' is accurate.

OECD caveat (3 June 2026): the OECD forecast UK growth of 0.9 per cent for 2026 (and 1.1 per cent for 2027) - a projection rather than a clear-cut upgrade, so bracketing 'the IMF and the OECD' as both having 'upgraded' the UK slightly overstates the OECD half.

Single-quarter caveat (Full Fact / ONS): Full Fact's government-tracker rates the 'highest sustained growth in the G7' pledge as 'appears off track', noting one strong quarter is not sustained leadership; and the UK economy contracted by 0.1 per cent in April 2026, immediately after the quarter Starmer cited, so the momentum did not carry into the next month.

Data caveat (ONS/OECD): the Q1 G7 comparison rests on OECD data retrieved on 13 May 2026, subject to differing cross-country revision policies, and Japan had not yet published its Q1 figure - so the 'fastest in the G7' ranking, while correct as reported, could shift on revision.
entries.confidence: High. The 0.6 per cent UK Q1 2026 growth figure and the G7 ranking (US 0.5, Canada 0.4, Germany 0.3, Italy 0.2, France 0.0, Japan outstanding at ~0.4) come directly from the ONS GDP first-quarterly-estimate bulletin (January to March 2026), corroborated by the House of Commons Library GDP international-comparisons briefing and The London Economic's G7 write-up; the per-head 0.6 per cent (fastest in four years) is from the ONS; the IMF's upgrade from 0.8 to 1.0 per cent is from its May 2026 update, and the OECD's 0.9 per cent from its 3 June 2026 forecast. The single-quarter caveat and the April 2026 -0.1 per cent contraction come from Full Fact's government-tracker on sustained G7 growth. Starmer's verbatim wording is reported in the Yorkshire Post PMQs write-up and the GOV.UK Downing Street readout. The main judgement call is the band: the specific claim - fastest-growing in the G7 in Q1 2026 - is true, correctly measured and correctly sourced, so it is well above half-true, but it cherry-picks one quarter that was followed by a contraction and the 'upgraded by the IMF and the OECD' element is only half-right (IMF yes, OECD a forecast not a clear upgrade), which keeps it in Mostly True rather than the True band. The one soft spot is that Japan had not reported its Q1 figure and the OECD comparison data is subject to revision, though on current figures the UK ranking holds.
