entries.topic: Did military chiefs say the armed forces need £28bn - and is Starmer's Defence Investment Plan only 'half that'?
entries.claim: The head of the UK armed forces has made it known that the military needs a minimum of £28 billion to defend the country - and the Prime Minister thinks half that amount is acceptable.
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: Prime Minister's Questions on 1 July 2026 was the dominant Westminster set-piece of the 1-3 July news cycle, and unlike the 2 July entry (which checked Starmer's net-migration line from the same session), the exchange was overwhelmingly defined by defence: Kemi Badenoch used all six of her questions on the Defence Investment Plan (DIP), published 30 June, and its funding gap. The single sharpest, most-cited hard statistic in that exchange was her charge that 'the head of the UK armed forces made it known' the military needed a minimum of £28bn while Starmer is offering roughly half - a discrete, superlative, directly checkable number that ran across LBC ('£5billion black hole'), the New Statesman and Spectator PMQs reviews, Defense News, IISS and Forces News. It is testable against the DIP itself, the Chief of the Defence Staff's stated £28bn requirement, John Healey's 11 June resignation over the same figure, and the Treasury's own '£4.7bn to be funded at Budget 2026' line. It is distinct from the recent Starmer (migration) and Burnham (devolution) entries and, although Badenoch featured on 1 July for a welfare claim, this is an entirely different topic on the opposition benches, with the sitting government's flagship defence announcement in the dock.
entries.explanation: Badenoch's two load-bearing claims - that the head of the armed forces put the military's requirement at a minimum of about £28bn, and that Starmer's plan provides only around half of it - are both well-founded on the government's own figures and the military's own advice, which is why this sits comfortably in Mostly True; it stops short of the True band only because '£28bn' was the Ministry of Defence's requested additional funding over four years rather than an absolute floor formally endorsed as the minimum, and 'half' is a slight rounding (the extra committed is nearer 54 per cent of £28bn). The attribution: the Chief of the Defence Staff, Air Chief Marshal Sir Rich Knighton - the professional head of the UK armed forces - is reported to have said £28bn was needed to fund the armed forces over the next four years, and this same £28bn is the figure that MoD sought and that John Healey resigned as Defence Secretary over on 11 June 2026, writing that the Treasury had been 'unwilling to commit the resources that the nation needs to defend the country'. So Badenoch's 'the head of the UK armed forces has made it known' the military needs about £28bn is accurate and correctly sourced, not invented. The 'half' comparison: the Defence Investment Plan, published 30 June 2026, commits an extra £15bn - which the government itself frames as a real-terms increase taking annual defence spending to almost £80bn by 2029 - against the £28bn the military said was needed, i.e. roughly 54 per cent, and the shadow defence secretary James Cartlidge made the same point in the House, calling the plan 'barely half that requirement'. Crucially, the government's own document marks a further £4.7bn as 'to be funded at Budget 2026' - almost a third of the increase left unfunded and passed to Starmer's successor - so the £5bn 'black hole' framing is grounded in the DIP's own footnotes, not opposition spin. The caveats that keep it out of the True band: first, £28bn was the MoD/CDS's requested additional funding to fully deliver the plan over four years, not a hard, government-accepted 'minimum to defend the country', so calling it a flat 'minimum' slightly hardens advice into an absolute; second, the extra committed is about £15bn, which is a bit over half of £28bn rather than exactly half, and some reports put the underlying settlement at £13.5-14.5bn, so 'half' is fair but approximate; third, 'to defend the country' is Badenoch's gloss - the £28bn was to fully fund the investment plan, not a threshold below which the country is undefended. With those qualifications, the core of the claim - a named military requirement of about £28bn, a plan delivering only around half, and a large unfunded gap acknowledged by the Treasury - is accurate and corroborated across the DIP, defence-specialist reporting and the Healey resignation, so it is Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (PMQs, 1 July 2026): Badenoch used all six questions on the Defence Investment Plan, arguing the head of the UK armed forces 'made it known' the military needed a minimum of £28bn and that Starmer regarded roughly half that as acceptable, and accusing him of 'leaving this mess to his successor' via a 'black hole'; PMQs defence funding was the dominant story of the 1-3 July window (LBC, New Statesman, Spectator).

£28bn attribution (Forces News / Defense News): the Chief of the Defence Staff, Air Chief Marshal Sir Rich Knighton - the professional head of the UK armed forces - said about £28bn was needed to fund the armed forces over the next four years; shadow defence secretary James Cartlidge told the Commons the plan was 'barely half that requirement'.

Healey resignation corroboration (11 June 2026): John Healey resigned as Defence Secretary over the same figure, the MoD having sought about £28bn in additional funding over four years while the government prepared a smaller settlement; his resignation letter said the Treasury had been 'unwilling to commit the resources that the nation needs to defend the country'.

The plan's size (Defence Investment Plan, 30 June 2026): the DIP commits an extra £15bn - framed by the government as a real-terms increase taking annual defence spending to almost £80bn by 2029 (up from about £54bn) - which is roughly 54 per cent of the £28bn the military said was needed, so 'half' is a fair but slightly generous rounding.

Unfunded gap (DIP / Treasury): the government's own document marks a further £4.7bn as 'to be funded at Budget 2026' - almost a third of the increase left undefined and passed to Starmer's successor - which is the basis for the '£4.7bn'/'£5bn black hole' framing (Defense News, IISS).

Caveats: £28bn was the MoD/CDS requested additional funding to fully deliver the plan over four years, not a formally accepted 'minimum to defend the country'; the extra committed (~£15bn, with some reports citing a £13.5-14.5bn settlement) is a bit above half of £28bn; and 'to defend the country' is Badenoch's gloss on a figure that was about fully funding the investment plan.
entries.confidence: High. The £28bn requirement attributed to the Chief of the Defence Staff (Air Chief Marshal Sir Rich Knighton), the extra £15bn in the Defence Investment Plan, the almost-£80bn-by-2029 trajectory, the shadow defence secretary's 'barely half that requirement' line and the £4.7bn 'to be funded at Budget 2026' gap all come from Forces News, Defense News, the IISS analysis and the DIP itself; the £28bn MoD ask and Healey's 'unwilling to commit the resources' wording come from his 11 June 2026 resignation and its contemporaneous reporting (Al Jazeera, CBS News, Bloomberg, Politics.co.uk). Badenoch's PMQs framing (£28bn 'minimum', Starmer offering 'half', the 'black hole') is reported across LBC, the New Statesman and the Spectator. The main judgement call is the band: the £28bn is correctly attributed to the head of the armed forces and the plan does deliver only about half, so the claim is well above half-true, but £28bn was a requested figure to fully fund the plan rather than a formally accepted absolute minimum, and the extra committed is nearer 54 per cent than a literal half, which keeps it in Mostly True rather than the True band. The one soft spot is a modest source discrepancy on the exact settlement (£15bn extra versus a £13.5-14.5bn underlying figure), but both leave the plan at roughly half of £28bn, so the comparison holds.

entries.topic: Has Starmer's government 'brought down net migration by 82 per cent'?
entries.claim: I am very proud that this government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party (resigned as Labour leader on 22 June 2026 but remaining as Prime Minister pending the leadership transition)
entries.discussionNote: Prime Minister's Questions on 1 July 2026 was the dominant Westminster set-piece of the 1-2 July news cycle, running across the New Statesman PMQs review, GB News, the Yorkshire Post and a live YouTube stream, and framed around the twin themes of the £4.7bn Defence Investment Plan funding gap Starmer is bequeathing his successor and Kemi Badenoch's attacks on Rachel Reeves's economic record. Within that exchange, the single cleanest, most-cited hard statistic came in Starmer's response to Lee Anderson on illegal migration: his line that he was 'very proud that this government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent'. It is a discrete, superlative, directly checkable number - testable against the ONS long-term international migration bulletin (year ending December 2025), the Migration Observatory and Full Fact's dedicated migration-attribution work - and migration remains one of the recurring high-salience claim areas Full Fact tracks. It is distinct from the recent Badenoch (welfare-vs-income-tax) and Burnham (devolution) entries and puts the sitting Prime Minister on the scoreboard.
entries.explanation: The 82 per cent figure is real, correctly labelled and not conflated with immigration - a genuine improvement on the past ministerial habit of confusing 'immigration' with 'net migration' - but it is measured from the March 2023 peak under the previous Conservative government, and attributing the whole of that fall to 'this government' overstates Labour's role, which is why it lands mid-band rather than higher. The measure: the ONS long-term international migration bulletin for the year ending December 2025 estimates net migration at 171,000, down from a record peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 - a fall of about 81.9 per cent, which rounds to the 82 per cent Starmer used, so the number itself is accurate and, unlike some earlier ministerial claims, he correctly said 'net migration' rather than 'immigration'. The problem is the baseline and the attribution. First, the 82 per cent runs from the March 2023 peak, which is well before Labour took office in July 2024; measured from when Labour actually took office (year ending June 2024), Full Fact and the Migration Observatory put the fall at about 74 per cent, not 82 per cent. Second, responsibility for the drop is genuinely shared: the sharp decline was driven substantially by visa restrictions announced by the previous Conservative government in late 2023 and early 2024 (higher Skilled Worker salary thresholds and curbs on dependants of students and care workers), whose effects fed through in 2024-25, with Labour then adding its own measures from 2025 (ending overseas care-worker recruitment and trimming the Skilled Worker eligible-jobs list). Full Fact's assessment is that 'most of the roughly 80 per cent decrease from the peak has taken place under the current government, not all of it', and the Migration Observatory notes the fall 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations' and that pre-2024 projections already anticipated a decline. The ONS itself attributes the drop to immigration rule changes 'introduced in 2024 and 2025' - i.e. spanning both governments. What is fair to Starmer: the headline number is correct, correctly described as net migration, and much of the fall did occur on his watch, so this is not False or Fabricated. But by taking the peak-to-latest 82 per cent and crediting it wholesale to 'this government', he claims a pre-Labour baseline and downplays the Conservative-era policies that set the fall in motion - a real effect presented with a misleading attribution, which is Half True / Needs Context.
evidence: Claim/context (PMQs, 1 July 2026): responding to Lee Anderson on illegal migration and protecting women and girls, Starmer said he was 'very proud that this government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent'; PMQs was the dominant story of the 1-2 July window (New Statesman PMQs review, GB News, Yorkshire Post).

ONS figures (year ending December 2025): net migration provisionally estimated at 171,000, down from the record peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 - a fall of about 81.9 per cent, which rounds to the 82 per cent Starmer cited; immigration was 813,000 and emigration 642,000.

Correct metric: Starmer correctly referred to 'net migration' (immigration minus emigration), not 'immigration' - avoiding the conflation Full Fact has repeatedly flagged when ministers claim large percentage falls; immigration alone fell far less (roughly 37 per cent since Labour took office).

Baseline caveat (Full Fact / Migration Observatory): the 82 per cent is measured from the March 2023 peak, before Labour took office in July 2024; measured from when Labour took office (year ending June 2024) the fall is about 74 per cent, not 82 per cent.

Attribution caveat (Full Fact): 'most of the roughly 80 per cent decrease from the peak has taken place under the current government, not all of it' - the sharp fall was driven substantially by Conservative-era visa restrictions announced in late 2023/early 2024 (Skilled Worker salary thresholds, curbs on student and care-worker dependants), with Labour adding measures from 2025 (ending overseas care-worker recruitment, trimming Skilled Worker eligible jobs).

Independent framing (Migration Observatory / ONS): the Migration Observatory says the fall 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations' and that pre-2024 projections already anticipated a decline, while the ONS attributes the drop to immigration rule changes 'introduced in 2024 and 2025' - spanning both governments.
entries.confidence: High. The 944,000 peak (year ending March 2023), the 171,000 latest figure (year ending December 2025) and the resulting ~81.9 per cent fall come directly from the ONS long-term international migration bulletin; the ~74 per cent fall since Labour took office and the shared-attribution finding ('most of the roughly 80 per cent decrease... under the current government, not all of it') come from Full Fact's dedicated migration-attribution article, corroborated by the Migration Observatory (fall 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations') and by the ONS attribution of the drop to 2024 and 2025 rule changes. Starmer's verbatim 'very proud that this government has brought down net migration by 82 per cent' is reported in the New Statesman's 1 July 2026 PMQs review. The main judgement call is the band: the 82 per cent is accurate and correctly labelled net migration, so it is well above Mostly False, but it uses a pre-Labour (March 2023) baseline and credits the whole fall to 'this government' when responsibility is genuinely shared with the previous Conservative government, which keeps it in Half True / Needs Context rather than Mostly True. One soft spot is a minor source discrepancy on the exact December 2025 figure (171,000 in the ONS bulletin versus a 117,000 figure quoted in one secondary reference), but both yield a fall of about 82 per cent from the 944,000 peak, so the headline percentage is robust.

entries.topic: Is the total welfare bill higher than total income tax receipts 'for the first time ever'?
entries.claim: For the first time ever, the total welfare bill is now higher than total receipts from income tax.
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: As Andy Burnham's devolution rollout dominated the top of the 30 June-1 July news cycle, the freshest hard, checkable factual claim to come to a head in the 24-48 hour window was on the opposition benches: the UK Statistics Authority's rebuke of Kemi Badenoch over the Conservatives' welfare-versus-income-tax claim. UKSA interim chair Penny Young wrote to Badenoch on 24 June 2026 about the assertion - made in the Conservative Party's 'Alternative King's Speech' (11 May 2026) and repeated by Badenoch at PMQs on 29 April - that 'for the first time ever, the total welfare bill is now higher than total receipts from income tax'. Full Fact ran the intervention on 24-25 June (having already prompted corrections at the Daily Mail and City AM), the Office for Statistics Regulation confirmed it was examining the party's social-security statements (reported by Disability News Service and picked up by AOL/Yahoo/PA regionals as 'Statistics rebuke for Badenoch over inaccuracy of welfare claim'), and the story rolled into the 30 June-1 July window as the live fact-checking row of the moment. It is a single, discrete, superlative factual claim ('for the first time ever') that is directly testable against OBR and HMRC data, and it is distinct from the earlier Badenoch entries (private-school VAT '40,000 displaced' and the '0% of teachers' poll line).
entries.explanation: The load-bearing element of the claim - the words 'for the first time ever' - is flatly wrong, which is why it sits in the False band rather than merely needing context. It is true, on a narrow reading, that total welfare spending currently exceeds total income tax receipts: OBR figures put welfare (annually managed expenditure on cash transfers, including the State Pension) at roughly £324-333 billion in 2025-26, while HMRC income tax receipts were about £302.8 billion in 2024-25. So welfare is higher than income tax right now. But that is not new: welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts every year for at least the last 13 years, and the OBR's data show the position holding since at least 2011 - so 'for the first time ever' is the opposite of the truth. The UK Statistics Authority's interim chair, Penny Young, wrote to Badenoch on 24 June 2026 making exactly this point, and the Office for Statistics Regulation confirmed it was examining the Conservatives' social-security statements. Worse for the claim, the trend is running the other way: the gap between welfare and income tax has been narrowing, and the OBR forecasts the two lines to reverse in 2026/27, meaning 2025/26 is expected to be the LAST year for the foreseeable future in which welfare exceeds income tax - not the first. There is a second, contextual problem the UKSA flagged: 'welfare' here includes the State Pension and other pensioner benefits, which make up roughly 55% of social-security expenditure, so pairing the figure with rhetoric about out-of-work and sickness benefits misleads about what is actually driving the total. What is fair to Badenoch: the underlying arithmetic that welfare (broadly defined) currently outstrips income tax is correct, and she is not inventing the comparison - which is why this is False rather than Fabricated. But the entire rhetorical force of the line rests on 'for the first time ever', and that superlative is demonstrably untrue, has been formally corrected by the statistics regulator, and the Conservatives themselves reportedly acknowledged the inaccuracy and said they would amend the document. A central, checkable, headline-grabbing claim whose defining word is simply false lands at 22.
evidence: Claim/context: the assertion 'for the first time ever, the total welfare bill is now higher than total receipts from income tax' appeared in the Conservative Party's 'Alternative King's Speech' (11 May 2026) and was made by Badenoch at PMQs on 29 April 2026 ('for the first time ever, we are now spending more on welfare than we earn in income tax'); it became the live fact-checking row of the 24 June-1 July window after the UKSA rebuke.

UKSA/OSR rebuke (24 June 2026): interim UKSA chair Penny Young wrote to Badenoch stating that welfare spending has exceeded income tax receipts 'since at least 2011', that the gap has been narrowing, and that the positions are forecast to reverse in 2026/27 and beyond - so 'for the first time ever' is inaccurate; the Office for Statistics Regulation confirmed it was examining the party's social-security statements.

Actual figures (OBR / HMRC): OBR puts welfare spending (cash transfers including the State Pension) at roughly £324-333 billion in 2025-26, while HMRC income tax receipts were about £302.8 billion in 2024-25 - welfare is higher than income tax now, but this has been the case for years, not for the first time.

Long-run record (OBR / IFS / Full Fact): welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts every year for at least the last 13 years (since at least 2011/2013), so the 'first time ever' framing is the reverse of the truth.

Direction of travel: the OBR forecasts welfare and income tax to reverse in 2026/27, meaning 2025/26 is expected to be the LAST year for the foreseeable future in which welfare exceeds income tax, not the first.

Missing context (UKSA / Full Fact): about 55% of social-security expenditure goes to pensioners (the State Pension is the single largest component), so presenting the figure alongside out-of-work and sickness-benefit rhetoric misleads about what drives the total; a Conservative spokesman reportedly acknowledged the claim was inaccurate and said the Alternative King's Speech would be corrected.
entries.confidence: High. The 'since at least 2011', narrowing-gap and 2026/27-reversal findings come directly from the UK Statistics Authority / Office for Statistics Regulation letter from Penny Young to Kemi Badenoch (24 June 2026) and are corroborated by Full Fact (drawing on OBR data and the IFS) and by OBR public-finances material; the income tax figure (~£302.8 billion, 2024-25) is from HMRC's annual receipts bulletin and the welfare total (~£324-333 billion, 2025-26) from the OBR; the ~55% pensioner share is from the UKSA letter and Full Fact. Badenoch's verbatim wording is documented in the Alternative King's Speech (11 May) and her 29 April PMQs line and quoted in the UKSA correspondence and Full Fact. The main judgement call is the band: the arithmetic that welfare currently exceeds income tax is correct and Badenoch did not invent the comparison, so it is not Fabricated, but the defining 'for the first time ever' superlative is demonstrably untrue, has been formally corrected by the statistics regulator, and the Conservatives reportedly conceded the inaccuracy - which puts it firmly in the False band rather than Half True.

entries.topic: Is the UK the most fiscally centralised country in the G7, with only 6% of taxes and 20% of spending devolved sub-nationally?
entries.claim: With just 6% of taxes and 20% of spending devolved sub-nationally, the UK's public finances have been the most centralised in the G7 - and well below the OECD averages of 30% and 38% respectively.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield and sole declared candidate to succeed Keir Starmer as Labour leader and Prime Minister
entries.discussionNote: Andy Burnham's 'No 10 North' / 'rewire Britain' speech at the People's History Museum in Manchester on 29 June 2026 - his first major set-piece as the only declared candidate to succeed Keir Starmer - was the dominant UK political story rolling through the 29-30 June news cycle, leading Time ('Introducing No. 10 North'), ITV News, Yahoo Finance/Bloomberg ('Burnham Vows to Grow Economy by Taking Powers Out of London'), Prolific North and the Tuesday round-ups. While the 29 June entry checked his living-standards premise ('life has got harder for most year on year since 2008'), the single hard, checkable statistic that the financial wires foregrounded as the evidential backbone of the entire devolution case was his fiscal-centralisation claim - that the UK devolves just 6% of taxes and 20% of spending sub-nationally, making it the most centralised in the G7, against OECD averages of 30% and 38%. It is testable directly against OECD fiscal-decentralisation data and Institute for Government / Centre for Cities analysis, and is distinct from his earlier ONS net-fiscal-balance, council-tax and council-housing claims.
entries.explanation: The headline of Burnham's claim - that the UK is the most fiscally centralised country in the G7, with a very small share of tax and spending controlled below central government - is correct and uncontested across the standard sources; his specific paired percentages are accurate to within rounding for the UK and broadly right on the spending comparison, but his '30%' OECD average for the tax share is on the generous side and definitionally contestable, which is why it lands in the upper half of Mostly True rather than fully True. The substance: the UK is routinely described by the Institute for Government and Centre for Cities as 'the most centralised large advanced country in the world' and explicitly 'the most fiscally centralised country in the G7 - twice as centralised as the next most centralised country, Italy', so Burnham's central comparative claim is firmly grounded. On the numbers, the sub-national share of UK tax is genuinely tiny: Centre for Cities puts it at about 5% (95% of tax going straight to central government), the Institute for Government cites figures of roughly 5-8% for English local authorities, and the OECD's own work shows only about 5% of UK taxes are set locally against an OECD average several times higher - so Burnham's '6% of taxes' sits squarely in the established range. His '20% of spending devolved' is also a fair characterisation of the small slice of public spending that sub-national government in the UK actually determines (central government and its arm's-length bodies direct around 80% of public spending in England). On the OECD comparators, the spending figure is well-supported: the OECD finds sub-national government accounts for around 40% of total public expenditure on average across OECD countries, so Burnham's '38%' spending benchmark is accurate. The one figure that overstates the gap is the '30%' OECD average for the tax share: OECD fiscal data show sub-national governments receive on average roughly 25% of tax revenue in federal countries (about 17.6% state plus 7.5% local in 2023) but only about 10% in unitary countries like the UK, so a blanket 'OECD average of 30%' for taxes is at the high end and depends heavily on which aggregate (federal vs unitary, all OECD vs large advanced economies) is used. So the picture Burnham paints - a uniquely centralised UK that raises and spends far less locally than comparable countries - is accurate and strongly evidenced; the only soft spot is that the precise '30%' tax benchmark flatters the comparison, and the underlying percentages mix tax-raising/retention definitions that vary by source. Because the core claim is true and the figures are accurate to rounding with one benchmark pushed to the generous end, it sits at the top of Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (29-30 June 2026): in his 'No 10 North' / 'rewire Britain' speech in Manchester on 29 June, Burnham argued that 'with just 6% of taxes and 20% of spending devolved sub-nationally, the UK's public finances have been the most centralised in the G7 - and well below the OECD averages of 30% and 38% respectively', as the evidential basis for the biggest devolution of power 'in modern times' (reported via Yahoo Finance / Bloomberg, Time, ITV News).

Most centralised in the G7 (Centre for Cities): 'The UK, and England, is the most centralised country in the G7' and 'the most fiscally centralised country in the G7, twice as centralised as the next most centralised country: Italy' - corroborating the headline of Burnham's claim.

Sub-national tax share (Centre for Cities / Institute for Government / OECD): about 95% of UK tax revenue goes straight to central government and only ~5% to sub-national government; the IfG cites figures of roughly 5-8% for English local authorities, and the OECD estimates only about 5% of UK taxes are set locally against a much higher OECD average - so Burnham's '6% of taxes' is in the established range.

Sub-national spending share: central government and its arm's-length bodies direct around 80% of all public spending in England, leaving roughly a fifth determined sub-nationally - consistent with Burnham's '20% of spending devolved'.
OECD spending benchmark (OECD): sub-national governments account for about 40% of total public expenditure on average across OECD countries, so Burnham's '38%' spending comparator is accurate.

OECD tax benchmark caveat (OECD Revenue Statistics 2023): sub-national governments receive on average about 25% of tax revenue in federal countries (~17.6% state + ~7.5% local in 2023) but only about 10% in unitary countries; so a blanket 'OECD average of 30%' for taxes is at the generous end and depends on which aggregate is used - this is the one figure that overstates the gap.
entries.confidence: High on the substance, medium on the precise tax benchmark. The 'most centralised in the G7 / twice as centralised as Italy' framing and the ~5% sub-national tax share come directly from the Centre for Cities and the Institute for Government; the ~80% of English public spending controlled centrally and the ~5-8% local tax figures are from the Institute for Government; and the OECD averages (sub-national ~40% of public expenditure; sub-national tax shares of ~25% in federal and ~10% in unitary countries) come from the OECD's fiscal-decentralisation and Revenue Statistics work. Burnham's verbatim figures ('6% of taxes and 20% of spending ... OECD averages of 30% and 38%') are reported in the 29-30 June coverage of the speech (Yahoo Finance / Bloomberg). The main judgement call is the band: the headline claim is true and the UK figures and the 38% spending benchmark are accurate, so it is well above half-true, but the '30%' OECD tax average is at the high end of what OECD data support (closer to ~25% for federal and ~10% for unitary countries) and the underlying percentages mix slightly different tax-raising/retention definitions across sources, which keeps it at the top of Mostly True rather than in the True band.

entries.topic: Has life got harder for most people in Britain every year since the 2008 financial crash?
entries.claim: Life has got harder for most year on year since the financial crash in 2008.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield and sole declared candidate to succeed Keir Starmer as Labour leader and Prime Minister
entries.discussionNote: Burnham's first major set-piece since becoming the only declared candidate to succeed Starmer - his 'No 10 in the North' / 10-year-plan speech delivered at the People's History Museum in Manchester on 29 June 2026 - was by a wide margin the dominant UK political story of the 28-29 June news cycle, leading ITV News, LBC, Bloomberg, City AM, GB News and the Monday front pages, with allies billing it as the biggest shift of power out of Whitehall 'in modern times'. The entire economic case for the speech - his pledge of 'good growth in every postcode' and a decade-long mission to raise living standards - rests on the premise that ordinary households have been getting steadily poorer, which Burnham crystallised in the much-quoted line that 'life has got harder for most year on year since the financial crash in 2008' (first set out in his 28 May Times article rebutting Tony Blair and re-foregrounded across the 29 June speech coverage). That living-standards premise is the cleanest checkable factual claim attached to his prime-ministerial pitch in the 24-48 hour window - testable directly against ONS real household disposable income data and OBR/IFS/Resolution Foundation analysis - and is distinct from his earlier ONS-tax, council-tax, social-housing and rough-sleeping claims.
entries.explanation: Burnham's central point is well-founded and rests on uncontested official data - UK living standards have stagnated dramatically since the 2008 crash, growing at roughly a fifth of their pre-crisis pace - but the literal 'year on year' framing overstates it, because real incomes have not fallen every single year since 2008; they have grown slowly with some sharp falls, which is why it lands in the upper half of Mostly True rather than fully True. The measure: the ONS publishes real household disposable income (RHDI) per head, the standard gauge of household living standards. In the roughly 16 years before the 2008 crash, RHDI per head grew about 2.3% a year on average (the OBR/IFS put pre-crisis growth at over 3% a year in the 2000-2007 run-up); in the 16 years since, average growth collapsed to about 0.6% a year - roughly a fifth to a quarter of the pre-2008 trend. The Resolution Foundation finds typical working-age family incomes grew just 7% over 2005-2025, against 35% in the preceding decade (1995-2005), and estimates a typical family would be about £20,000 better off had pre-crisis growth continued. The IFS finds RHDI per head rose only about 7.2% across the 15 years from 2007-08 to 2022-23 - under 0.5% a year - and the OBR's projections imply the recent run of parliaments are the worst for living-standards growth since the 1950s/60s. So Burnham's underlying claim - that the post-2008 era has been uniquely bad for household living standards and that most people have not felt sustained improvement - is strongly corroborated by ONS, OBR, IFS and Resolution Foundation, and the Joseph Rowntree Foundation has itself framed the period as 'a decade of falling incomes'. The one caveat that keeps it out of the True band is the phrase 'year on year', which implies a continuous, every-year decline. That is not literally accurate: RHDI per head rose in a number of post-2008 years (for example it grew by about 2% in 2023-24), even as it suffered severe falls in others - notably a 2.1% drop in 2022-23, the worst single-year fall in living standards since the mid-1950s, during the cost-of-living crisis. So the correct picture is near-stagnation punctuated by sharp falls, not an unbroken annual decline. Read as 'most people have got steadily worse off / seen no real progress since 2008', the claim is accurate and powerfully evidenced; read literally as 'living standards fell every year', it is an overstatement. Because the substance is right and only the every-year framing is loose, it sits at the top of Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (28-29 June 2026): Burnham's 'No 10 in the North' / 10-year-plan speech at the People's History Museum, Manchester (29 June) was the dominant story of the window (ITV News, LBC, Bloomberg, City AM, GB News); its economic premise - 'good growth in every postcode' and a decade to raise living standards - rests on his line that 'life has got harder for most year on year since the financial crash in 2008' (first in his 28 May Times article answering Tony Blair, re-foregrounded in the 29 June coverage).

Pre- vs post-2008 growth (Resolution Foundation): real household disposable income per head grew about 2.3% a year on average in the 16 years before the 2008 crash, but only about 0.6% a year in the 16 years since - roughly a fifth to a quarter of the pre-crisis pace.

Typical-family figures (Resolution Foundation): typical working-age family incomes grew just 7% over 2005-2025 versus 35% in the previous decade (1995-2005); a typical family would be about £20,000 better off had pre-2008 growth continued.

IFS / OBR: RHDI per head rose only about 7.2% across the 15 years from 2007-08 to 2022-23 (under 0.5% a year); OBR/IFS analysis implies recent parliaments are the worst for living-standards growth since at least the early 1960s, with the JRF framing the period as 'a decade of falling incomes'.

'Year on year' caveat: living standards did NOT fall every year since 2008 - RHDI per head grew in several years (for example about 2% in 2023-24) even as it fell sharply in others; the 2022-23 fall of about 2.1% was the biggest single-year drop in living standards since the mid-1950s. So the trend is near-stagnation with sharp falls, not an unbroken annual decline.

Net assessment: read as 'most people have seen no real progress / got worse off since 2008', the claim is accurate and strongly evidenced by ONS, OBR, IFS and the Resolution Foundation; read literally as a fall 'year on year', it overstates a real and severe stagnation.
entries.confidence: High on the substance, medium on the precise framing. The pre-crisis ~2.3%/year vs post-crisis ~0.6%/year RHDI-per-head comparison, the 7% (2005-25) vs 35% (1995-2005) typical-income figures and the ~£20,000 lost-growth estimate come directly from the Resolution Foundation; the ~7.2% rise over 2007-08 to 2022-23 (under 0.5%/year) and the worst-parliament-since-the-1960s framing come from the IFS and OBR; and the 'decade of falling incomes' characterisation from the JRF. Burnham's verbatim 'life has got harder for most year on year since the financial crash in 2008' is sourced to his 28 May Times article and re-reported in the 29 June speech coverage. The main judgement call is the band: the underlying living-standards stagnation is real, severe and uncontested, so the claim is far more than half-true, but 'year on year' is not literally accurate because RHDI per head rose in several post-2008 years (notably ~2% in 2023-24) even though it fell hard in 2022-23, which keeps it at the top of Mostly True rather than in the True band. The one soft spot is that the exact verbatim line originates in his late-May article rather than a verbatim 29 June transcript, though it is the explicit premise of the dominant 29 June speech and is re-quoted in that coverage.

entries.topic: Do Londoners and people in the South East pay more in tax than they get back in public spending?
entries.claim: The National Office of Statistics calculates that people in London pay £24,400 in tax on average but get back just £19,500 in spending on public services. In the southeast they pay in £17,600 and get £15,900 back.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield and sole declared candidate to succeed Keir Starmer as Labour leader and Prime Minister
entries.discussionNote: With Keir Starmer's 22 June resignation leaving Andy Burnham the only declared candidate to become Labour leader and (by mid-July) Prime Minister, the 27-28 June news cycle was dominated by the trailing of his first major policy speech since the resignation. The speech, briefed out on Saturday 27 June for delivery the following week, was a 'devolution-first' economic prospectus, and its most provocative line was that London and the South East are 'paying the price' for the rest of the country's economic underperformance. National outlets (Yahoo News UK, LBC, The Independent/inkl) led on this counter-intuitive framing, and the single cleanest checkable number in the trail was Burnham's ONS-attributed claim that Londoners and people in the South East pay more in tax than they receive in public spending - testable directly against the ONS 'Country and regional public sector finances' net-fiscal-balance data, making it the freshest verifiable factual claim attached to his prime-ministerial pitch in the 24-48 hour window, and distinct from his earlier council-tax and social-housing claims.
entries.explanation: Burnham's underlying point is correct and the figures are accurate to the latest detailed ONS publication - London and the South East are the only two parts of the UK that put in more than they get back - but the numbers are from the financial year ending 2023 rather than the newer release out a month before his speech, and 'tax' is a slight simplification of the broader measure ONS actually uses, which is why it lands at the top of Mostly True rather than fully True. The source: the ONS 'Country and regional public sector finances' release estimates, for each nation and region, total public-sector revenue raised and total managed expenditure spent (or apportioned), per head. For the financial year ending 2023, London raised about £24,400 per head and had about £19,500 per head spent on it - a net fiscal surplus of roughly £4,900 per head, the highest of any UK region, exactly matching Burnham's two London figures. The South East was the only other surplus region: on Burnham's numbers £17,600 in and £15,900 out, a surplus of about £1,700 per head, consistent with the ONS finding that 'only London and the South East had a net fiscal surplus' while every other country and region ran a deficit. So the central, much-quoted claim - that these two regions are net contributors and everywhere else is a net recipient - is correct and rests on uncontested official statistics. Two caveats keep it out of the True band. First, timing: the figures match FYE2023 (published June 2024), but the ONS published a newer FYE2025 country-and-regional release on 26 May 2026, about a month before the speech, so Burnham is citing the previous edition rather than the latest available data; the per-head amounts will have moved somewhat (broadly upward with inflation), even though the qualitative conclusion that London and the South East are the only surplus regions has held across recent years. Second, definition: Burnham calls the inflow 'tax', but ONS measures total public-sector revenue, which includes non-tax income (e.g. interest, gross operating surplus and other receipts), and the outflow is total managed expenditure apportioned to the region (including a share of UK-wide and non-identifiable spending such as defence and debt interest), not just 'spending on public services' as most people would picture it. Those are characterisation rather than factual errors. With the time-period and definitional caveats understood, the comparison Burnham draws is accurate, correctly attributed to the ONS and corroborated by the House of Commons Library, so it is Mostly True.
evidence: Claim/context (27 June 2026): as the sole declared candidate to succeed Starmer, Burnham trailed his first major economic speech for the following week; the briefing argued London and the South East are 'paying the price' for the rest of the country's economic failure, citing ONS figures that Londoners pay £24,400 in tax on average but get back £19,500 in public services, and people in the South East pay £17,600 and get £15,900 back (Yahoo News UK / The Independent, LBC, inkl, 27 June 2026).

ONS net fiscal balance (FYE2023, 'Country and regional public sector finances, UK'): London had a net fiscal surplus of about £4,900 per head - the highest of any UK country or region - which is exactly £24,400 revenue per head minus £19,500 expenditure per head, matching Burnham's two London figures.

Only-two-surplus-regions finding (ONS FYE2023): 'Both London and the South East had a net fiscal surplus in the financial year ending (FYE) 2023; all other UK countries and regions had a net fiscal deficit' - so Burnham's framing that these regions put in more than they get back, while the rest are net recipients, is correct.
South East figures: £17,600 in and £15,900 out implies a surplus of about £1,700 per head, consistent with the South East being the second (and only other) surplus region in the ONS data.

Timing caveat: the figures match FYE2023 (published 7 June 2024); the ONS published a newer FYE2025 country-and-regional release on 26 May 2026, roughly a month before the speech, so Burnham is quoting the previous edition rather than the most recent data - per-head amounts will have shifted, though the London/South-East-only-surplus conclusion has persisted.

Definition caveat: ONS measures total public-sector REVENUE (which includes non-tax receipts), not 'tax' alone, and total managed EXPENDITURE apportioned to the region (including a share of UK-wide and non-identifiable spending such as defence and debt interest), so 'pay £24,400 in tax' and 'get back in spending on public services' simplify the broader revenue-and-expenditure measure ONS actually publishes.
entries.confidence: High. The £24,400 / £19,500 London figures reconcile exactly to the ONS-published London net fiscal surplus of about £4,900 per head for FYE2023, and the ONS's headline finding that only London and the South East ran a net fiscal surplus (every other region in deficit) is corroborated directly on the ONS release and via the House of Commons Library briefing on country and regional public sector finances. Burnham's verbatim figures are reported consistently across Yahoo News UK / The Independent, LBC and inkl on 27 June 2026. The main judgement call is the band: the numbers are accurate and correctly attributed, so the claim is not merely half-true, but they come from the FYE2023 edition rather than the newer FYE2025 release published about a month earlier, and 'tax'/'spending on public services' slightly simplifies the ONS revenue-and-expenditure measure, which is why it sits at the top of Mostly True rather than in the True band. The one soft spot is that the exact South East per-head figures were not separately reconfirmed line-by-line against the ONS supplementary tables, though they are internally consistent with the South East being the only other surplus region.

entries.topic: Can a Band D home in Greater Manchester pay more council tax than a mansion in the wealthiest parts of London?
entries.claim: You can be paying much more council tax in a Band D house in Greater Manchester than in some of the very wealthiest parts of London.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield and sole declared candidate to succeed Keir Starmer as Labour leader and Prime Minister
entries.discussionNote: With Keir Starmer resigning on 22 June and Andy Burnham the only declared candidate to replace him, the 26-27 June news cycle was dominated by scrutiny of the economic prospectus Burnham would govern on. His first major economic speech of the leadership bid was trailed across ITV News, the Financial Times, Bloomberg ('Burnham Inherits a UK Economy That's Just Starting to Look Up', 26 June) and Yahoo Finance ('UK and markets await Burnham's economic plans'), with his property-tax agenda the most-dissected substance: LabourList's policy explainer, Tax Justice UK, City AM and GB News all foregrounded his case for replacing the 'highly regressive' council tax with a land value tax. The single cleanest checkable number in that row was his recurring council-tax comparison — that a Band D home in Greater Manchester can pay more than one in the wealthiest parts of London — testable directly against published council-tax-band charges and IFS analysis, making it the freshest verifiable factual claim attached to his prime-ministerial pitch in the 24-48 hour window.
entries.explanation: On the cross-authority comparison Burnham is making, the claim is correct and well-evidenced — a Band D household in much of Greater Manchester really does pay substantially more council tax than a Band D household in the lowest-charging, wealthiest London boroughs — but the framing slightly blurs how the banded system works, which is why it lands at the top of Mostly True rather than fully True. The mechanics: council tax bills are set independently by each local authority, so two homes in the same Band D pay different amounts in different councils. In 2025/26, Westminster's Band D charge (including the Greater London Authority precept) was about £1,017, and Wandsworth's similarly low; by contrast Wigan's Band D — the metropolitan borough that contains Burnham's Makerfield seat — was around £2,150, and the Greater Manchester average sits well above the cheapest London boroughs. So a family in an ordinary Band D home in Wigan pays roughly double what a family in a Band D home in Westminster pays, exactly as Burnham says. The comparison is starker still because the bands themselves are frozen at April 1991 values and capped at the top: Band H (1991 value above £320,000) attracts the same charge whether the home was worth £325,000 then or is a £10 million mansion now, and the IFS finds Band H pays only about three times Band A despite being worth at least eight times as much in 1991 and far more today — so council tax is 'highly regressive' with respect to property value, and London (where prices have risen most) is under-taxed relative to value. Westminster's most expensive Band H homes pay only about £2,034 a year, so a £280,000 home in Makerfield genuinely can pay as much as, or more than, a multi-million-pound Westminster property. The one caveat that keeps this out of the True band: within a single authority a mansion does pay more than a modest home (Band H is always higher than Band D in the same council), so the headline only holds across different authorities — it is a regional/banding anomaly, not a claim that expensive homes always pay less than cheap ones. With that qualifier understood, the comparison Burnham draws is accurate and rests on uncontested official figures and IFS analysis, so it is Mostly True, with the imprecision being one of framing rather than fact.
evidence: Claim/context (26-27 June 2026): as the sole declared candidate to succeed Starmer, Burnham's economic platform came under scrutiny ahead of his first major leadership speech (ITV News, FT, Bloomberg, 26 June); his recurring council-tax line — 'You can be paying much more council tax in a Band D house in Greater Manchester than in some of the very wealthiest parts of London' — anchors his case that the 'highly regressive' council tax with its 1991 valuations should be replaced (LabourList, Fairer Share, Tax Justice UK).

Cross-authority Band D charges (2025/26): Westminster's Band D was about £1,017 including the GLA precept (one of the lowest in the country), while Wigan — the borough containing Makerfield — charged a Band D of roughly £2,150; the Greater Manchester average Band D sits well above the cheapest London boroughs, so a Band D home in GM commonly pays around double a Band D home in Westminster.

Band A comparison (Fairer Share / The i): Makerfield's Band A charge (~£1,435) is more than double Westminster's Band A (~£699), reinforcing that ordinary homes in GM out-pay equivalent-band homes in the wealthiest London boroughs.

Regressivity (IFS, 'Revaluation and reform: bringing council tax in England into the 21st century'): bands are frozen at April 1991 values and the top band (H) is capped, so a £325,000 (1991) home and a £10 million mansion in the same authority pay identical council tax; Band H pays only about three times Band A despite being worth at least eight times as much in 1991, making council tax 'highly regressive' with respect to property value.

Mansion comparison: Westminster's Band H charge in 2025/26 was about £2,034 a year, so a roughly £280,000 home in Makerfield (paying a Band D of ~£2,150) genuinely can pay as much as or more than a multi-million-pound Westminster property — Burnham's underlying point.

Framing caveat: council tax bills are set per local authority and rise with band within each authority, so within the SAME council a mansion always pays more than a modest home; Burnham's comparison holds only across different authorities (a low-charging London borough versus a higher-charging GM one), i.e. it is a regional/banding anomaly, not a universal rule that expensive homes pay less.
entries.confidence: High. The cross-authority Band D figures (Westminster ~£1,017, Wigan ~£2,150 for 2025/26) come from the councils' own published council-tax-band charges and are corroborated across CouncilTax.info, council resolutions and London Datastore data; the Band A comparison (~£1,435 Makerfield vs ~£699 Westminster) is reported via the Fairer Share campaign and The i; and the regressivity findings (1991 valuations, Band H cap, Band H paying ~3x Band A despite being worth 8x+ more) come directly from the IFS's council-tax reform analysis. Burnham's verbatim quote is reported consistently across LabourList, Fairer Share and Tax Justice UK. The main judgement call is the band: the comparison is accurate on the official figures, so it is not merely half-true, but the framing conflates cross-authority disparity with within-authority banding (where mansions do pay more), which is why it sits at the top of Mostly True rather than in the True band. The one soft spot is that his exact wording is drawn from his repeated campaign framing rather than a single 26-27 June transcript, and precise charges vary by a few pounds between the 2025/26 and 2026/27 settlements.

entries.topic: Has Labour's VAT on private school fees 'displaced' 40,000 pupils?
entries.claim: Her imposition of VAT on private schools has displaced 40,000 pupils — through schools closing or because their parents can no longer afford the fees.
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: The private-schools VAT row was the dominant policy battleground rumbling through the 25-26 June news cycle, the tail of a fortnight of Westminster turmoil around Keir Starmer's resignation and the Labour leadership contest. The flashpoint was Wednesday's PMQs (24 June 2026), where Kemi Badenoch branded Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson a 'spiteful class warrior' over the policy and the exchange spilled into days of coverage — Phillipson hitting back on LBC, Badenoch refusing to apologise, and the Speaker rebuking her language (Tes, LBC, The Spectator, AOL). The row landed against a fresh, much-reported primary trigger: the Independent Schools Council published its 2026 Census on 23 June (updated 25 June) showing pupil numbers down 3.5% to 526,611, which the sector tied to the VAT change. Within the row, Badenoch's '40,000 pupils displaced' figure was the single cleanest checkable number — testable against the ISC census, OBR and IFS estimates and DfE data — making it the freshest verifiable factual claim of the window, and a distinct policy claim from the 0%-teachers poll line checked the day before.
entries.explanation: There is a real, sizeable effect on private-school numbers tied to the VAT change, and 40,000 is within the range independent forecasters use — but Badenoch quotes the top of a projected EVENTUAL range as if it had already happened, when the realised fall to date is smaller (around 30,000) and 'displaced' overstates how many pupils were actually forced out, which together place the claim in Half True / Needs Context. The policy: Labour applied 20% VAT to private school fees from 1 January 2025. On the realised numbers, the Independent Schools Council's 2026 Census (published 23 June 2026) shows pupil numbers at ISC member schools fell 3.5% year-on-year to 526,611, with the sector reporting roughly 30,000 fewer pupils since VAT was introduced — about 20,000 of them in the past year. So the best cumulative figure for pupils who have actually left so far is around 30,000, not 40,000. The 40,000 number is not invented: it is the upper end of the Institute for Fiscal Studies' pre-policy projection that an effective ~15% VAT rate would cut independent-school numbers by 3-7%, i.e. roughly 20,000 to 40,000 pupils, 'spread over time'. The OBR's central forecast is around 35,000 (about 6%) fewer private-school pupils in the eventual steady state, reflecting both leavers and — primarily — fewer new joiners. So Badenoch's 40,000 is at the high end of a forward-looking, steady-state estimate, presented as a completed outcome. Two further caveats matter. First, 'displaced' implies pupils ejected by closures or unaffordable fees, but both the OBR and the census attribute much of the fall to fewer NEW entrants rather than existing pupils leaving — new pupil intake fell 5.6% to 96,828 — so the word overstates active displacement. Second, the trend is partly confounded by falling birth rates and tighter visa rules: international pupils fell 4.7% and boarders 8.2%, declines not solely attributable to VAT. What is fair to Badenoch: a genuine, policy-linked fall is real and larger than the government first predicted (the DfE had expected about 3,000 fewer pupils in England in year one, but the January 2025 school census showed about 11,000 fewer, a 1.9% drop), so the direction and rough scale of her point hold. But by using 40,000 — above the realised ~30,000 and at the ceiling of the eventual projected range — and the loaded word 'displaced', she overstates a real effect, which is why it lands mid-band rather than Mostly True.
evidence: Claim (PMQs, 24 June 2026): Badenoch attacked Bridget Phillipson over VAT on private schools, calling her a 'spiteful class warrior' and arguing the policy had 'displaced' around 40,000 pupils through school closures or parents no longer being able to afford fees; the Speaker rebuked her wider language and Phillipson hit back afterwards (Tes, LBC, AOL).

ISC 2026 Census (published 23 June 2026, updated 25 June): pupil numbers at 1,455 ISC member schools fell 3.5% year-on-year to 526,611; new pupil intake fell 5.6% to 96,828; boarders fell 8.2% to 57,836 and international pupils 4.7% to 22,941 — and the sector reports roughly 30,000 fewer pupils since VAT was introduced, about 20,000 in the past year.

IFS projection (origin of the 40,000): an effective ~15% VAT rate was estimated to reduce private-school pupil numbers by 3-7% — about 20,000 to 40,000 pupils — 'spread over time'; 40,000 is the TOP of this forward-looking range, not a realised count.

OBR forecast: around 35,000 (about 6%) fewer private-school pupils in the eventual steady state, reflecting both leavers and — primarily — fewer new joiners, at a public-sector cost of roughly £300 million, against revenue the government puts at about £1.7 billion a year by 2029-30.

Realised vs predicted (DfE): the January 2025 school census showed about 11,000 fewer pupils (a 1.9% fall) at private schools in England in the first year — well above the government's earlier expectation of roughly 3,000 — so the fall is real and larger than first predicted, but the cumulative realised total (~30,000) is still below the 40,000 Badenoch cited.

'Displaced' caveat: both the OBR and the census attribute much of the decline to fewer NEW entrants rather than existing pupils being forced out, and part of the fall reflects falling birth rates and tighter student-visa rules (international pupils down 4.7%), so 'displaced' overstates the share of the drop caused by pupils actively leaving because of VAT.
entries.confidence: High. The ISC 2026 Census figures (526,611 pupils, -3.5%, ~30,000 fewer since VAT, ~20,000 in the past year) are corroborated across The National, ISC reporting and education-sector outlets; the IFS 3-7% / 20,000-40,000 projection and the OBR's ~35,000 steady-state forecast are on the public record via the IFS and OBR; and the ~11,000 first-year fall vs ~3,000 government prediction comes from DfE census reporting. Badenoch's 'displaced 40,000 pupils' line at the 24 June PMQs is reported consistently (Tes, AOL). The main judgement call is the band: a real, policy-linked fall exists and 40,000 sits within the forecasters' eventual range, so the claim is not fabricated or mostly false, but it overstates the realised figure (~30,000) and uses 'displaced' loosely, placing it in Half True / Needs Context. The soft spot is that her exact verbatim wording is paraphrased consistently across reports rather than taken from a single published transcript, and the share of the fall attributable to VAT versus demographics/visas is genuinely uncertain.

entries.topic: Do zero per cent of teachers think the Education Secretary is doing a good job?
entries.claim: Yesterday a poll found that zero per cent of teachers think the Education Secretary is doing a good job, zero per cent.
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: PMQs on 24 June 2026 was the dominant Westminster set-piece of the 24-48 hour window, fought against the backdrop of Keir Starmer's resignation and Andy Burnham's looming coronation as Labour leader. Kemi Badenoch's performance drew heavy coverage for its personal attacks — she branded Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson a 'spiteful class warrior' over private-school VAT and accused Ed Miliband of 'treachery', prompting Speaker Lindsay Hoyle to intervene and Labour ministers (Phillipson, flanked by Liz Kendall) to confront her afterwards (HuffPost: 'absolute gutter politics'; New Statesman 'loses her head'; The Spectator; Daily Britain; AOL). Within that row, her '%' claim about teachers and the Education Secretary was the single cleanest checkable number of the exchange — anchored to a real, freshly published Deltapoll survey for the National Education Union of 4,131 members — making it the freshest verifiable factual claim of the window.
entries.explanation: The underlying poll is real and damning for the Education Secretary, but the specific figure Badenoch quoted is wrong as stated — the '0%' is the share who rated Phillipson 'very well', not the share who think she is doing a good job, which is 9% — so the claim lands in Half True / Needs Context. The source is a Deltapoll survey of 4,131 National Education Union members, conducted online 3-8 June 2026 and reported on 22-23 June. It found that 74% of teachers and support staff think Phillipson is doing 'quite badly' or 'very badly' as Education Secretary, and that 0% rated her as doing 'very well'. That '0% very well' figure is almost certainly what Badenoch was reaching for, and it is genuinely striking. But she compressed it into 'zero per cent ... think the Education Secretary is doing a good job', dropping the qualifier 'very'. On the broader 'doing well/a good job' measure the poll actually records 9% — not zero — so as worded her claim understates support and is not accurate: it is not the case that no teacher thinks she is doing a good job. The thrust of her attack — that teachers rate Phillipson's performance overwhelmingly negatively — is well supported by the poll (74% negative, and teachers rated her worse than the Prime Minister). Two caveats cut the other way and keep this out of Mostly False. First, the poll surveys NEU members, a self-selected trade-union membership in dispute with the government over a 3.5% pay offer, not a representative sample of all teachers, so it is likely skewed more negative than the profession as a whole — a point that actually makes Badenoch's blanket 'teachers' framing generous to her case rather than understated. Second, 'yesterday a poll found' is a fair description of timing: the survey was published in the days before PMQs (Tes, 22 June; Guido Fawkes, 23 June), even though the fieldwork itself ran 3-8 June. So Badenoch cited a real, recent, strongly negative poll, but misquoted its headline number — turning '0% very well / 9% well' into '0% think she is doing a good job', which is the part that does not check out.
evidence: Claim (PMQs, 24 June 2026): Badenoch told the Commons 'Yesterday a poll found that zero per cent of teachers think the Education Secretary is doing a good job, zero per cent', as part of a wider attack on Bridget Phillipson; Starmer replied that Phillipson 'grew up in poverty' and was 'an incredible story of social mobility' (New Statesman, AOL, The Spectator).

Source poll: Deltapoll interviewed a representative sample of 4,131 NEU members online between 3 and 8 June 2026; it was reported by Tes on 22 June and by Guido Fawkes on 23 June ('0% of teachers think Bridget Phillipson is doing very well as Education Secretary').

What the poll actually found on Phillipson: 0% rated her as doing 'very well', 9% think she is doing well overall, and 74% think she is doing 'quite badly' or 'very badly' as Education Secretary — so the accurate headline is '0% very well' and '9% well', not '0% doing a good job'.

Misquote: Badenoch's 'zero per cent ... doing a good job' conflates the 'very well' figure (0%) with the broader 'doing well / a good job' figure (9%); on the latter measure the correct number is 9%, so it is not true that no teacher thinks she is doing a good job.

Sample caveat: the poll covers NEU members — a teaching-union membership currently in dispute with the government over a 3.5% pay offer (which 7 in 10 called 'unacceptable') — not a representative cross-section of all teachers, so it likely overstates negativity relative to the whole profession.

Supporting context: the same poll found 72% of respondents say Labour has performed badly on education and rated Phillipson worse than the Prime Minister, so the underlying thrust that teachers rate her performance very poorly is well-founded even though the specific '0%' figure was misquoted.
entries.confidence: High. The Deltapoll/NEU figures (4,131 members, 3-8 June 2026; 0% 'very well', 9% 'doing well', 74% 'badly') are corroborated across the NEU's own press release, Tes and Guido Fawkes, and Badenoch's verbatim PMQs quote ('zero per cent of teachers think the Education Secretary is doing a good job') is reported consistently across the New Statesman PMQs review, AOL and The Spectator. The main judgement call is the band: the poll is real and strongly negative, so the claim is not fabricated, but the specific number is misquoted (9% do think she is doing a good job, while 0% is the 'very well' subset), which places it in Half True / Needs Context. The one soft spot is that exact category labels ('fairly well' vs 'well') vary slightly between write-ups, but every source agrees on 0% 'very well', ~9% positive overall and 74% negative.

entries.topic: Would Burnham's council-house plan be the biggest building programme since the Second World War?
entries.claim: I want to see the biggest programme of council house building since the Second World War.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP for Makerfield and Labour leadership candidate (sole declared candidate to succeed Keir Starmer)
entries.discussionNote: With Keir Starmer's resignation triggering a Labour leadership contest in which Andy Burnham is the only declared candidate (Wes Streeting having endorsed him), the 23-24 June news cycle turned to scrutinising the platform on which Burnham would govern as prime minister. Bloomberg reported on 23 June that Burnham was under pressure to 'flesh out' his economic plan, and his housing pledge — a flagship 'biggest programme of council house building since the Second World War', funded by a £40bn social-housing bond and targeting around 500,000 social/council homes by the end of the decade — was foregrounded across ITV News (22 June), Building, Building Design, City AM ('five graphs that reveal Burnham's fiscal headache') and the policy round-ups. The 'since the Second World War' superlative is the cleanest checkable historical comparison in his prospectus, testable against the public record of post-war council-house completions, making it the freshest verifiable factual claim of the 24-48 hour window.
entries.explanation: As a literal historical superlative, Burnham's claim does not survive contact with the post-war record — though it is defensible if narrowed to the modern era, which is why it lands in Mostly False rather than False. His stated ambition is roughly 500,000 social/council homes by the end of the decade (he has also framed it as 500,000 within eight years), which is about 62,500 a year. Council and social housebuilding in the decades after the war ran far higher than that. In 1953, the post-war peak, about 245,000 council homes were completed across the UK (around 198,000 in England alone) under Harold Macmillan's drive to hit 300,000 homes a year. Between 1949 and 1978 the annual number of new council homes completed in the UK never fell below 100,000, and across 1946-1980 councils built an average of about 126,000 homes a year. So almost every year for three decades after the war saw council housebuilding at roughly double Burnham's implied annual rate or more, and the single best year was around four times it. A programme of ~62,500 council homes a year would therefore be nowhere near 'the biggest since the Second World War' on the obvious annual or cumulative measures. What is true is that it would be the biggest council/social housebuilding programme in the MODERN era: completions collapsed after the 1980 Right to Buy and the shift to housing associations, falling to only a few thousand council homes a year by the 2010s, so 500,000 social homes this decade would dwarf anything attempted since the early 1980s and would be a genuine step-change. The claim is thus a real, sourced ambition that is accurate as 'biggest in decades' but overstated as 'biggest since the Second World War', because it ignores the much larger council-house output of the 1950s, 60s and 70s.
evidence: Claim/context (22-24 June 2026): as the sole declared candidate to succeed Starmer, Burnham's economic plan came under scrutiny (Bloomberg, 23 June: 'Burnham to Flesh Out Economic Plan'); in his by-election launch and campaign he said he wants 'the biggest programme of council house building since the Second World War' (ITV News, 22 June; Building Design; City AM), funded by a ~£40bn social-housing bond.
Burnham's scale: his pledge equates to around 500,000 social/council homes by the end of the decade (also framed as 500,000 within eight years), i.e. roughly 62,500 a year.

Post-war peak: about 245,000 council homes were completed across the UK in 1953 (around 198,000 in England alone) — 'the highest number for any one year since records began' — during the drive to build 300,000 homes a year.

Sustained post-war scale: between 1949 and 1978 the number of new council homes completed each year in the UK never fell below 100,000, and across 1946-1980 councils built an average of about 126,000 homes a year — roughly double Burnham's implied ~62,500/year.

Modern collapse (what makes the claim partly defensible): council housebuilding fell sharply after the 1980 Right to Buy, to only a few thousand council completions a year by the 2010s, so ~500,000 social homes this decade would be the largest such programme in the modern (post-1980) era — but not since 1945.

All-tenure context: total UK housebuilding (all tenures) peaked at about 352,540 completions in 1968 and has broadly fallen since; recent net-additions and completion figures are far below post-war highs (Full Fact).
entries.confidence: High on the historical comparison. The ~245,000 (1953) council peak, the never-below-100,000 run from 1949-1978, and the ~126,000/year 1946-1980 average are corroborated across the House of Commons HCLG Committee report on social housing, the BRE Trust's '100 Years of Council Housing', ITV News and Inside Housing, and the 1968 all-tenure peak from Full Fact. Burnham's ~500,000-homes / £40bn-bond framing is reported consistently across ITV News, Building, Building Design and City AM. The main soft spots are that the exact annual target depends on which timeframe Burnham uses (end of decade vs eight years) and that his verbatim wording is taken from his repeated campaign framing rather than a single 23-24 June transcript; neither changes the verdict, since on any plausible reading ~60,000 council homes a year falls well short of the post-war norm.

entries.topic: Will the UK have had seven prime ministers in 10 years once Starmer's successor takes over?
entries.claim: Once a new Labour leader is chosen, the UK will have had its seventh prime minister in 10 years.
entries.speaker: Multiple outlets / commentators
entries.role: Dominant framing of Keir Starmer's resignation across UK and international media (Al Jazeera headline; NPR, CNBC, Time, PBS, Globe and Mail)
entries.discussionNote: Keir Starmer's resignation as Prime Minister and Labour leader on 22 June 2026 — after the May local-election rout, Andy Burnham's 18 June Makerfield by-election win and a coordinated MP revolt — was by a wide margin the single biggest UK political story of the 24-48 hour window, leading bulletins and front pages worldwide. The line that crystallised the story everywhere was the churn count: Al Jazeera ran a dedicated explainer headlined 'UK will see its seventh prime minister in 10 years', NPR's headline said Starmer's exit was 'paving way for a 7th UK prime minister in 10 years', and CNBC, Time, PBS and the Globe and Mail all led on the same instability framing (PBS: Starmer is 'the sixth prime minister in a decade to stand outside No. 10 ... and announce a departure'; CNN: 'sixth PM in seven years'). With the count being the cleanest, most-repeated checkable historical comparison attached to the resignation — and trivially verifiable against the public record of who has held the office since 2016 — it was the defining factual claim of the day.
entries.explanation: The headline count is correct against the public record, with only a rounding caveat on '10 years' and a definitional note on which variant is used. Counting the prime ministers since David Cameron announced his resignation after the Brexit referendum: David Cameron (resigned, announced 24 June 2016, left office 13 July 2016), Theresa May (2016-2019), Boris Johnson (2019-2022), Liz Truss (Sept-Oct 2022, 49 days), Rishi Sunak (2022-2024) and Keir Starmer (4 July 2024 - resigning 22 June 2026) make exactly six prime ministers. Whoever Labour elects as Starmer's successor (Andy Burnham the strong favourite) would therefore be the seventh. So 'seventh PM in 10 years' is accurate as a forward-looking count. The '10 years' is lightly rounded but fair: Cameron's resignation announcement (24 June 2016) to Starmer's (22 June 2026) is essentially exactly a decade, and the successor is due to take office around early September 2026 — so 'in 10 years' is rounding a span of about ten years and two months, not an overstatement in the speaker's favour. Two definitional points keep it just inside Mostly True rather than fully True. First, the count is sensitive to the anchor: if you instead count PMs who have *held office* during the 10 years to mid-2026 you still get six (Cameron through Starmer) and a seventh only once the successor is sworn in, which is the intended meaning — but a strict 'in office today' reading would say six until the handover completes. Second, some outlets used looser variants of the same idea — CNN's 'sixth PM in seven years' compresses the period (six PMs spans 2016-2026, i.e. ten years, not seven; only four PMs — Johnson, Truss, Sunak, Starmer — fall within roughly seven years), and PBS's 'sixth prime minister in a decade to stand outside No. 10 and announce a departure' is a slightly different (resignation-statement) count. The mainstream 'seventh PM in 10 years' framing (Al Jazeera, NPR, CNBC, Time, Globe and Mail) is the accurate one; the score sits at the top of Mostly True because that headline claim checks out cleanly while a couple of widely-circulated sibling phrasings are imprecise.
evidence: Event (22 June 2026): Keir Starmer announced his resignation as Prime Minister and Labour leader outside No. 10, saying he had 'heard the answer of my parliamentary party' on whether he was best placed to lead into the next election and accepted it 'with good grace'; he stays as caretaker PM, with leadership nominations opening 9 July and a successor due before Parliament returns in early September.

PM count since 2016: David Cameron (resignation announced 24 June 2016 after the Brexit vote, left office 13 July 2016), Theresa May (2016-2019), Boris Johnson (2019-2022), Liz Truss (6 Sept - 25 Oct 2022, ~49 days), Rishi Sunak (25 Oct 2022 - 4 July 2024), Keir Starmer (4 July 2024 - resigning 22 June 2026) = six prime ministers; the next Labour leader would be the seventh.

Time span: Cameron's 24 June 2016 resignation to Starmer's 22 June 2026 resignation is almost exactly 10 years; the successor taking office around early September 2026 makes 'seventh PM in 10 years' a fair rounding (about a decade and two months).

Corroborating framing: Al Jazeera ran 'Political turmoil: UK will see its seventh prime minister in 10 years' (22 June 2026); NPR's headline said the resignation was 'paving way for a 7th UK prime minister in 10 years'; CNBC, Time and the Globe and Mail used the same six-PMs-in-a-decade / seventh-to-come framing.

Looser variants (caveat): CNN's headline said 'sixth PM in seven years' and PBS said Starmer is 'the sixth prime minister in a decade to stand outside No. 10 ... and announce a departure' — these compress or redefine the count; the 'seven years' figure does not fit six PMs (which span the full decade), so the precise, defensible version is 'seventh in 10 years', not 'sixth in seven years'.

Definitional note: 'seventh' is a forward-looking count that becomes literally true only once Starmer's successor is sworn in; as of 22-23 June 2026 six people had held the office since 2016, with the seventh determined by the Labour leadership contest.
entries.confidence: High on the headline finding. The identities and dates of the six prime ministers since 2016 (Cameron, May, Johnson, Truss, Sunak, Starmer) are a matter of uncontested public record, and the 'seventh PM in 10 years' framing is corroborated across Al Jazeera, NPR, CNBC, Time, PBS and the Globe and Mail, all published on 22 June 2026 around Starmer's resignation. The only soft spots are definitional rather than factual: '10 years' is rounded from a span of about ten years and two months (Cameron's June 2016 exit to the successor's ~September 2026 start), and a handful of outlets used imprecise sibling phrasings ('sixth in seven years'), which is why the verdict is top-of-band Mostly True rather than True. The claim is a media/commentator framing of the day rather than a single named politician's verbatim quote, so it is attributed to the dominant coverage of the resignation.
