entries.topic: Young people and disability benefits
entries.claim: Mild anxiety is not a reason to be on disability benefit — it just isn't. We can't afford it, it can't continue.
entries.speaker: Nigel Farage
entries.role: Leader, Reform UK; MP for Clacton
entries.discussionNote: Farage's welfare reform push has dominated the week alongside the Nowak/two-tier policing row. This specific claim — that 'mild anxiety' should not qualify for disability benefits — was posted on his official Facebook and repeated in ITV and GB News interviews, drawing strong reactions from disability rights groups and Labour. Reform's pledge to cut £40bn from the welfare bill makes the factual basis of this claim central to the political debate.
entries.explanation: There is a genuine and well-documented rise in disability benefit claims for mental health conditions, particularly among young people — the number of 16-to-24-year-olds awarded PIP per month has tripled, and mental health conditions now account for over 35% of PIP claims, up from about 25% a decade ago. In that sense Farage is pointing at a real trend. However, 'mild anxiety' is a misleading characterisation of who receives PIP: the assessment process requires claimants to demonstrate significant functional limitations in daily living or mobility, and the vast majority of successful mental health claimants have moderate-to-severe conditions, often with comorbidities. PIP is not awarded for a diagnosis alone but for its impact on daily life. The framing implies the system hands out benefits for trivial conditions, which misrepresents how PIP assessments work.
evidence: DWP PIP statistics (January 2026): 3.9 million people claiming PIP in England and Wales, with psychiatric disorders the most common condition category (39% of all PIP claimants).
Centre for Social Justice analysis: 633,000 people were claiming PIP for anxiety and mood disorders as of July 2025, with almost 250 new awards per day in this category.
The number of 16-to-24-year-olds receiving PIP per month has tripled from 2,967 to 7,857.
IFS research: more than half the rise in 16-to-64-year-olds claiming disability benefits since the pandemic is due to mental health or behavioural conditions.

PIP eligibility is based on functional impact, not diagnosis: claimants must score points across daily living and mobility activities, meaning 'mild' cases that do not significantly affect functioning would not typically qualify.
ONS data: almost one million 16-to-24-year-olds are neither in work nor training, with over half citing ill-health.
entries.confidence: High — DWP PIP statistics, Centre for Social Justice analysis and IFS research all corroborate the underlying trend; PIP eligibility criteria are published by DWP.

The Conservative Party's 2024 manifesto claimed £12bn in welfare savings, and the Resolution Foundation called even that 'extremely challenging to implement'. Reform has published no detailed breakdown of how £40bn would be achieved, and the party's own policy documents list broad aspirations (tighter checks, fraud reduction, restricting benefits to UK citizens) without costings. Independent welfare analysts have not validated this figure.
evidence: Reform UK claims £40bn in annual welfare savings from tighter eligibility, fraud reduction and administrative reform — no independent costing published.
OBR scored the Labour government's welfare reforms at £1.9bn savings by 2030/31 (Gov.uk, March 2026).
IFS: changes to the government's benefit reforms reduced projected savings by £3bn in 2029/30.
The Conservative Party's 2024 manifesto claimed £12bn in welfare savings; the Resolution Foundation called this 'extremely challenging to implement'.
Total welfare spending in 2025/26 was approximately £333bn (OBR). A £40bn cut would represent a 12% reduction — a scale unprecedented in modern UK welfare history.
Reform's policy page lists broad headings (restrict welfare to UK nationals, tighter PIP assessments) but provides no line-by-line costings.
entries.confidence: High — OBR, IFS and Resolution Foundation analyses provide clear benchmarks against which the £40bn claim can be assessed. No independent body has endorsed the figure.

entries.topic: Welfare spending vs income tax revenue
entries.claim: The UK has crossed a line. Welfare spending: £333 billion. Income tax revenue: £331 billion. We're now spending more on welfare than we raise in income tax. This is a first in our history.
entries.speaker: Luke Evans
entries.role: MP for Hinckley and Bosworth
entries.discussionNote: The claim that welfare spending has overtaken income tax 'for the first time ever' went viral across social media in early June 2026, shared by Conservative and Reform UK politicians, UKIP, and commentators. Full Fact published a detailed rebuttal on June 4, making it one of the most fact-checked claims of the week. It was amplified alongside the broader debate about Reform UK's welfare reform agenda.
entries.explanation: The headline figures are broadly correct: OBR data for 2025/26 shows welfare spending of approximately £333bn against income tax receipts of approximately £331bn. However, the claim that this is 'a first in our history' is wrong. Full Fact found that welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts in every year since at least 2013/14 — making this at least the thirteenth consecutive year, not a watershed moment. Furthermore, the OBR forecasts that income tax receipts will exceed welfare spending again in 2026/27. The misleading framing — presenting a longstanding pattern as a sudden crisis — is what makes the claim mostly false despite the underlying numbers being accurate.
evidence: OBR Economic and Fiscal Outlook (March 2026): welfare spending £333bn, income tax receipts £331bn in 2025/26.
Full Fact (4 June 2026): welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts every year since at least 2013/14. The 'first time ever' framing is false.
OBR forecasts income tax receipts will overtake welfare spending in 2026/27 for the first time since at least 2013/14.
Welfare spending includes the state pension (approximately £130bn), which is the single largest component — not just working-age benefits.
The claim was repeated by UKIP, multiple Conservative MPs and Reform UK figures, all using the 'first time' framing that Full Fact debunked.
entries.confidence: High — figures verified directly against OBR data; Full Fact's analysis provides definitive context on the historical pattern.

entries.topic: Two-tier policing in Britain
entries.claim: It is now clear to growing millions in this country that we are living under two-tier policing.
entries.speaker: Nigel Farage
entries.role: Leader, Reform UK; MP for Clacton
entries.discussionNote: Farage's 'two-tier policing' claim at PMQs on 3 June, delivered in the wake of the Henry Nowak murder conviction and the Southampton riots, was the single most discussed political moment of the week. It provoked direct rebukes from the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition, Baroness Lawrence, the London Mayor, and later the US State Department. It trended on social media, dominated front pages, and triggered a parliamentary row.
entries.explanation: Farage's claim presents an assertion of systemic, deliberate bias in policing as established fact. The available evidence does not support this. The Home Affairs Committee's report on the police response to the Southport riots found no evidence of two-tier policing. The Independent Office for Police Conduct is investigating the specific conduct of officers in the Nowak case, but one tragic case of apparent individual failure does not demonstrate a systemic policy of differential treatment by ethnicity. Moreover, the statistical evidence on policing disparities points in the opposite direction to Farage's implication: Home Office stop-and-search data for the year ending March 2025 shows Black people were stopped at 3.8 times the rate of White people, and Asian people at a higher rate than White people — the reverse of the anti-white bias Farage alleges. The Nowak case raises serious questions about the officers involved, but it is not evidence of a nationwide two-tier system.
evidence: Home Affairs Committee report on police response to Southport riots: found no evidence of two-tier policing in officers' handling of violence and criminality.

Home Office stop-and-search statistics (year ending March 2025): Black people stopped at 3.8 times the rate of White people; 53,949 searches of Black individuals vs 287,307 of White individuals despite Black people comprising a far smaller share of the population.
IOPC opened an investigation into the conduct of officers who handcuffed Henry Nowak — an investigation into individual officers, not a finding of systemic bias.
Henry Nowak's father Mark Nowak told reporters: 'We do not want his death to be used to create further division, hatred or tension.'
The claim was rejected by the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition (Kemi Badenoch), Baroness Lawrence, and the Deputy Prime Minister (David Lammy).
entries.confidence: High — Home Affairs Committee findings, Home Office stop-and-search statistics and IOPC statements all directly relevant. No official body or independent review has found evidence of systemic two-tier policing as Farage describes.

With his approval ratings at a 50-year low for a PM at this stage, the 'fastest-growing economy in the G7' boast is the most-scrutinised factual claim in UK politics this week, amplified by Bloomberg, GB News and broadsheet coverage.

entries.topic: Cost of scrapping net zero
entries.claim: Scrapping net zero would save tens of billions and help fund roughly £70bn a year of tax cuts.
entries.speaker: Richard Tice
entries.role: Deputy Leader, Reform UK
entries.explanation: Independent analysts dispute that abandoning net zero produces savings on this scale, and the wider £70bn tax-cut costing is contested. Several net-zero programmes are projected to create jobs and investment rather than only impose costs.
evidence: Reform's manifesto proposes ~£70bn/year in tax cuts, partly funded by cancelling net-zero goals — a costing contested by independent analysts.
Government Clean Energy Jobs Plan projects 800,000 clean-energy jobs by 2030; TUC Cymru warned Reform's stance risks ~40,000 manufacturing jobs in Wales.
entries.confidence: Medium — claim direction well documented; savings figures contested. Reconfirm against costings.

entries.topic: Size of the NHS waiting list
entries.claim: Around 7 million people are on the NHS England waiting list.
entries.speaker: Wes Streeting
entries.role: Then Health Secretary
entries.explanation: This conflates cases with people. There are about 7.4 million cases (treatments) on the list but only about 6.3 million people, because many are waiting for more than one treatment. Quoting ~7 million people overstates the number of individuals affected.
evidence: NHS England (Jan 2025): ~6.3 million people on the list, ~7.4 million cases/treatments.
Full Fact flagged the health secretary repeatedly overstating the number of people.
entries.confidence: High — per Full Fact and NHS England statistics.
